data.title: Ranked: The best and worst UK cities for public transport
data.description:
Rapid transit was born in the United Kingdom, with the opening of the Metropolitan Railway in 1863, followed by lines in Liverpool in 1886 and Glasgow in 1896. Since that heady period, only one new city has received a dedicated urban network, with the Tyne and Wear Metro, serving Newcastle, Gateshead and Sunderland, opening in 1980.

Metro rail systems have been repeatedly tabled by local authorities, and ruled out, in Belfast, Sheffield, Bristol, Cardiff, Cambridge, Birmingham and Manchester. In December 2025, the planned £2.5bn West Yorkshire Mass Transit tram scheme connecting Leeds and Bradford was postponed indefinitely. The highly anticipated, long-stalled £17bn Northern Powerhouse Manchester-Liverpool rail line has also been shunted into the hazy future.

Buses remain the chief mode for moving locals, and visitors, around UK cities. Manchester’s city-owned Bee Network is expanding, with fares capped on all buses, trains and trams, and free electric buses operating in the city centre. The model is London, where buses were never deregulated and the number of journeys has risen from 1.2 billion in 1986 to 1.8 billion today. Elsewhere, usage has dive-bombed.

Cars still play a role in UK cities and self-drive cabs could be the biggest disruptors yet. Clean-air charging zones have been introduced in London, Bath, Birmingham, Bradford, Bristol, Portsmouth, Sheffield, and Tyneside. However, London is still ranked 14th cleanest in Europe by the Clean Cities Campaign and not a single UK city appears in the global top 20 of cycling cities, assessed by the Copenhagenize Index (Bristol, Manchester, Glasgow and London are, at least, in the top 100).

It is, then, a mixed picture. Here’s my take on 12 transport systems for major cities in the UK, ranked from worst to best. Please let us know your thoughts and share your experiences in the comments.

12. Bristol

My verdict: 2/10

Forums, news reports and rankings concur that the gateway-city to the west of England is easier to whizz through than to explore.

In 2018 – after 12 years of local government dithering – MetroBus was launched as an affordable alternative to trams. High-spec guided buses would fly along dedicated busways around Bristol and the surrounding area, providing rapid transit for a fraction of the cost of a tram network. To date, more than £230m has been spent developing the scheme, and while it generates fewer angry headlines than a couple of years ago, the buses are often late, slow moving, overcrowded and are considered expensive by local users and students.

Bristol has no metro-type rail service, with GWR trains stopping at only a handful of suburban stations. Strike action by drivers of First buses in September 2025 caused widespread disruption, but was suspended in October following a pay settlement.

In 2008, Bristol was named as the UK’s first Cycling City, backed by £11.4m of government funding, and is still regarded as a good city for cyclists, despite its steep slopes. Oh, and its clogged roads: Bristol was the second most congested city in the UK in transport and traffic tech firm Inrix’s 2025 survey.

11. Sheffield

My verdict: 3/10

The Sheffield Supertram is a light rail system with 50 stations and three main lines (Blue, Yellow, Purple) connecting the city centre with the universities and suburbs like Hillsborough and Crystal Peaks. Fares for 2026 have been capped at £3 for the first time in 25 years, pandemic years excluded.

But as recently as December, the service was in crisis mode, with the Sheffield Tribune reporting: “As passengers will know, over the last few weeks, the service has been terrible. So bad, in fact, that the mayor and his team went out to tram stops last Friday to apologise and give people free tickets and chocolate.”

Locals, fed up with cancellations due to broken overhead lines and staff shortages, are giving up on the light railway service – brought back under public control in 2024 – instead returning to buses. But evening bus services have seen patchy performance, too, while some areas are poorly served and estate-touring routes take forever. The South Yorkshire Mayoral Authority aims to run a publicly controlled bus network from September 2027.

Last July, the Government “paused” plans to electrify more of the Midland Mainline, making this Britain’s largest city without electrified rail travel.

10. Leeds

My verdict: 4/10

It’s often claimed – especially by Yorkshire’s politicians – that Leeds is the largest city in Europe without a metro, tram or light rail. But the departures board at the main rail terminal is packed with commuter-type services and Northern runs 15 separate lines out of the station. Residents of Ilkley, Keighley, Castleford and Skipton benefit from regular trains to the city.

West Riding is a constellation of cities and towns, and there are regular and frequent trains to Wakefield, Sheffield and Hull. Nonetheless, according to the Centre for Cities: “Although Leeds and Marseille have comparable populations… the French city has around 776,000 residents who can access the city centre by public transport in 30 minutes or less, while 299,000 people can reach Leeds city centre under the same criteria.” That is, admittedly, not great.

West Yorkshire came 39th out of 39 for bus services in Transport Focus’s 2024 bus passenger satisfaction survey – for the second year running: “Arriva in West Yorkshire was the lowest rated bus operator with 69 per cent of passengers satisfied with their journeys.”

9. Glasgow

My verdict: 5/10

The third oldest underground system in the world – after London and Budapest – the Glasgow Metro opened in 1896. Sadly, the 6.5-mile network – if that’s even the word – hasn’t been expanded since, and so the vast majority of the 1.85 million people who call metropolitan Glasgow home live nowhere near a station.

Perhaps the track could do with some modernising, too, as cold weather led to two days of power outages earlier this month. In 2024, the network’s iconic “Clockwork Orange” trains, introduced in the Eighties, were replaced by new, modern, wheelchair-accessible trains, as part of a £288m system upgrade – which will also feature driverless trains, updated signalling, and platform screen doors.

On the access front, Centre for Cities approves: “All large British cities, except Glasgow, have worse public transport accessibility than their European peers”.

Most Glaswegians rely on the bus, but the network is generally rated so-so – with the main operator, First Bus, scoring a dismal 1.5 stars on Trustpilot. “Absolutely shocking service, this company is the definition of bare minimum,” said one local user.

8. Manchester

My verdict: 5.5/10

Whatever you think of Andy Burnham as a metro mayor, you have to admit he’s good at marketing. He has turned championing the Bee bus network into a powerful political vehicle. Manchester began taking over its buses in 2023, a process completed on January 5 – the first city to do so in the UK, following 40 years of deregulation.

Joint tram and bus travel is now 20 per cent cheaper with a new AnyBus + tram ticket. Adult fares were capped at £2 for all Greater Manchester bus services in 2022. Fifty new electric buses operate in the first franchised area, with hundreds more due to come over the next few years. Buses will run earlier, later and more frequently, with integrated bus services connecting passengers with first and last tram and train services.

The missing link in the masterplan, though, is Lancashire: metro systems really need to reach beyond official city borders and some services into the county are slow, unreliable and infrequent. The Ordsall Chord, opened in 2017, has helped connect central Manchester rail stations, but draughty, smelly, diesel-heavy Victoria still feels like a blast from the past.

Other bad smells are the ego-shrivelling withdrawal of HS2 and more than £100m in taxpayer funding blown on the cancelled clean air zone in Greater Manchester. The city and its sprawling suburbs consistently rank worst or near to the worst in air pollution surveys.

7. Birmingham

My verdict: 6/10

Britain’s second most populous city has no dedicated rail or underground trains. The West Midlands Railway – which actually extends to Manchester and Hereford – is the local name for West Midlands Trains, which also operates to London Euston and Liverpool Lime Street under a separate brand name – London Northwestern Railway. But there is a fairly frequent service between Birmingham and the main hubs at Wolverhampton, Walsall and Coventry.

A single 15-mile tram line connects Wolverhampton and Birmingham and the system is being expanded to 80 stops as part of a £1.3bn investment programme. Birmingham’s bus and coach connections were ranked second in transport co-ordination firm CMAC’s 2024 ranking of the best UK cities for public transport, with the judges highlighting “the city’s focus on sustainable and efficient transport solutions, such as introducing low-emission buses and real-time tracking technology.”

6. Belfast

My verdict: 6.5/10

When the Northern Irish capital’s new Grand Central station opened in September 2024, it was welcomed by locals – the old “integrated terminal” at the Europa Bus Centre was pretty awful – but universally described as not at all grand. Certainly, compared with the New York original, and many European city termini, the sawtooth glass structure is visually underwhelming. Frequent trains operate to Bangor, but most rail services are inter-city, leaving every 20 minutes or so, or every hour to Dublin.

There are a few stops inside the city limits, but the main metro system is the extensive network of pink city buses, operated by Translink, covering 12 main routes with a high level of frequency, connecting the city centre to suburbs and beyond. These 12 splinter into lettered branches, which means less frequency if you’re going further away. There’s also the Glider BRT service, which consists of two routes operated by articulated buses, using 15 miles of dedicated bus lanes.

5. Cardiff

My verdict: 7/10

The “Welsh Tube” is coming! The South Wales Metro, a £1bn transport system to be run by Transport for Wales, should be up and running from spring 2026, with 105 miles of largely overground travel, linking the Welsh capital with visitor attractions at Caerphilly and Barry Island.

Greener, quieter tram-trains will run on six electrified, colour-coded lines, with new stations, better access and a contactless payment system. The network should deliver a train every 15 minutes from Merthyr, Aberdare and Treherbert to Cardiff, and a train every 3-4 minutes from Pontypridd to Cardiff. Journey times from the top of each valley into Cardiff will also be reduced by around 15-20 minutes, making journeys faster.

Plans are also pushing forward for Cardiff Crossrail, an on-street tram service linking Cardiff Central with Cardiff Bay. It hopes to be operational when the city plays host to the 2028 European Football Championship. Cardiff’s buses are fair to middling, but are criticised for being hub-and-spoke; going in and out of the city is easy, but anywhere else difficult to impossible.

4. Liverpool

My verdict: 7.5/10

With just six underground stations and two lines, Liverpool’s dedicated metro – born in 1886 – may first appear very wee indeed. But the 68 stations covering 75 miles are part of one of the most heavily used railway networks in the UK outside London.

Merseyrail’s network reaches out to Southport, Ormskirk, the Wirral and Chester, with Northern and other firms running to St Helens, Warrington and Manchester on the so-called “City line”. It’s a highly rated system, topping Transport Focus satisfaction rankings in 2025.

In 2023, a new station, Headbolt Lane, was built at Kirkby, but onward journeys to Wigan and Blackburn involve a change. Most UK rail operators lease their trains, a largely unreported spanner in the works for so-called nationalisation. But Merseyrail’s new Class 777 trains are owned by the Liverpool City Region Combined Authority, marking a shift towards direct ownership for future fleet upgrades and expansion.

Buses in Liverpool were rated above Manchester’s in the Transport Focus 2024 passenger satisfaction survey, but still only 28th out of 39. Given all the hoo-ha around the Bee network, we can expect Liverpool to rebrand its buses any day now...

Since December, Avanti trains to and from London have been stopping every hour at Liverpool South Parkway, allegedly to ease trips to the airport. But that’s a two-mile walk with your luggage. For now, the bus is better.

3. London

My verdict: 8/10

The Elizabeth Line, which opened in 2022 and cost £19bn, has put a spring in the step of London’s commuters, especially those living on an east-west axis. Ongoing improvements on the six lines of the London Overground network, which has expanded hugely since its launch in 2007, have eased movements outside the central zone.

Old Tube trains on the Bakerloo and Piccadilly lines are still unpleasant when jam-packed, but a lot of rolling stock is being replaced. Work from home and less footfall on Fridays has made the Tube less of a sweating-sardine experience.

With around 700 bus routes (servicing a network that was, uniquely for the UK, never fully deregulated), fast and frequent trains to outlying dormitory towns (under 50 mins to Peterborough) and the nation’s only international rail link, London is by many miles the most extensive and best-funded urban transport system in the UK.

It’s £3,264 for a six-zone annual travelcard; a chunk of money, for sure, but driving is a no-no these days and, in any case, according to Inrix, London is the most congested city in Europe.

2. Nottingham

My verdict: 8.5/10

Assorted rankings for “least car-dependent city” have often praised Nottingham, due to its extensive bus network and 20 miles of tram lines.

Nottingham City Transport scored the highest customer satisfaction score of any city operator in the country in the UK Bus Operator of the Year 2024; it was the sixth time the city was given the title. Transport Focus’s Your Bus Journey survey for 2024 also praised Nottinghamshire and Greater Nottingham, which came sixth and seventh in its list of 39 local authorities.

“Nottingham City Council has concentrated its efforts on improving the overall network for passengers... [including] better bus stops and shelters and faster journey times,” it said.

The Robin Hood pay-as-you-go card can be used across Nottingham’s bus, tram and train network, making it simple and hassle-free to get around Nottingham. A range of ticket types is available, including a paper day-ticket bought on board buses or at tram stops. Locals want the trams to be expanded and the home of Raleigh and Sturmey-Archer gears remains a work in progress for cyclists, but this city seems to be moving forward on several right paths.

1. Edinburgh

My verdict: 9/10

In 2015, the Border Railways line between Edinburgh and Tweedbank opened, the first new domestic passenger service in a century. It’s very scenic, like so many journeys in Scotland.

Edinburgh lacks a metro rail network, but does it need one? The Central Belt is pretty well served in most areas, and Edinburgh has decent connections to its main exurbs. For tourists, the city is eminently walkable, notwithstanding the slopes of Old Town. There are regular buses to attractions like the Royal Botanic Gardens, Edinburgh Zoo and Portobello Beach.

The tram line, which generated heaps of negative press before it opened in 2014 due to cost overruns, delays, a contract cancellation and disruption caused by roadworks, is now considered a major positive by most locals. With 23 stops over 11.5 miles, it connects Prince Street with Leith and the airport. Trams run every 7 minutes for most of the day, and every 10 minutes early mornings and evenings. They don’t run through the night, but the Lothian NightBus, launched in 1925, is legendary.

Ticket prices in Edinburgh are competitive – £2.20 for an adult single on the bus or tram anywhere in the city zone.

In March 2025, Time Out ranked Edinburgh among the world’s best for public transport, behind only London and Brighton in the UK. It reported that 80 percent of locals expressed “a positive view of its transport network”. The Edinburgh tram is much more enjoyable than the Tube, though, and, frankly, the views are better from its buses – so I have the Scottish capital pipping the English one.

Data sources: Transport Focus; Clean Cities Campaign; CMAC; Centre for Cities; Time Out; Copenhagenize; UK Bus Awards; Britain Remade; Inrix; Trustpilot.

Do you agree with our expert’s ranking? Let us know in the comments below.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Ladbrokes owner scraps Cheltenham race sponsorship over Labour tax raid
data.description:
The betting giant behind Coral and Ladbrokes is ending its 52-year sponsorship of Cheltenham’s Coral Cup, blaming the Government’s tax raid on bookmakers.

Entain said its decision to end the tie-up was because of the “significant increase” in gambling taxes announced by Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, at her Budget in November.

Cheltenham will now be left looking for a new backer for the race, with Entain understood to have provided a six-figure sponsorship sum for the deal which has been in place since 1974.

Simon Clare, of Entain, said: “The scale of the recent tax increase on betting operators means we must take difficult decisions to mitigate its impact.

“Ending our sponsorship after 52 years is incredibly regrettable but reflects the need to reassess where and how we invest under the new cost landscape.”

The Chancellor’s decisions to raise the general betting duty from 15pc to 25pc and to nearly double tax on online casinos from 21pc to 40pc both come into effect in April.

While horse racing was given an exemption from the betting duty increase, critics have argued the changes will hammer licenced operators such as Entain, which has forecast a hit of as much as £150m from the tax increases.

The move is the latest blow to the racing industry which is also at risk of being undermined by Labour’s attacks on the rural economy via a fox hunting ban.

At the same time, the gambling industry is facing a fresh setback after it emerged Lisa Nandy, the Culture Secretary, has drawn up plans to raise licence fees for gambling companies by as much as 30pc.

Documents mistakenly published online on Tuesday showed Ms Nandy was plotting the move which would amount to more than £8m in extra costs for bookmakers.

The row has deepened after the Department for Digital, Culture, Media and Sport warned that without the 30pc fee increase, the Gambling Commission would be forced to cut back its work tackling black market gambling.

Officials said that a lower increase in fees would force the regulator to make £15.8m of savings over the next six years, including a headcount reduction of 10pc and refocusing on its core statutory duties of licensing, compliance and enforcement.

The demand sparked a furious response from the gambling industry, which said the Government was forcing companies to pay to tackle a problem of its own making.

An industry source said: “Everyone wants a strong regulator but we’re now being asked to cough up millions to help the Gambling Commission police a black market threat Rachel Reeves has caused. The Government is knowingly sending customers to illegal operators.”

Entain said it will retain sponsorship of other races including the Ladbrokes King George, the Coral Eclipse, the Coral Welsh and Scottish Grand Nationals, Ladbrokes Ayr Gold Cup and Glorious Goodwood.

Mr Clare added: “Cheltenham will continue to be a major moment for our customers and we will promote the festival across all our channels as strongly as ever.

“However, with higher taxation and racing’s already-high cost base – driven by media rights and the statutory levy – it is essential that the sport and the betting industry work together to grow racing’s appeal while ensuring it remains financially sustainable.”

data.title: US is best place to invest, says boss of British drugs giant
data.description:
The boss of Britain’s largest drug developer has praised the US as the best place to invest amid fears over the waning appeal of the UK.

Dame Emma Walmsley, the outgoing chief executive of GlaxoSmithKline (GSK), said it was delusional to suggest Britain could become a competitor to the US. She lauded America as key to the group’s future growth.

“It’s more than half of GSK’s global turnover today,” she said of the US. “It’s still the leading market in the world in terms of the launches of new drugs and vaccines. It’s still, probably alongside China … the best market in the world to do business development.

“No one should be deluded that the UK is going to be a massive scale market, a domestic market, but it can be an exporter of innovation in life sciences.”

Dame Emma said GSK, which spends £1bn developing new drugs in the UK every year, is preparing to invest $30bn (£23bn) in the US by the end of this decade.

She is set to leave GSK at the end of the year after eight years in charge.

‘Worst country in Europe’ for drug prices

Her comments are likely to fuel fears about the attractiveness of Britain’s pharma sector after several major drugmakers cancelled UK projects worth billions – amid concerns over NHS drug price rules and pressure from Donald Trump, the US president.

AstraZeneca recently announced it was pausing a planned £200m investment in a Cambridge research facility, while Merck scrapped plans for a £1bn research centre in London and laid off 125 scientists.

In September, Eli Lilly, the American manufacturer of weight loss drug Mounjaro, also said that it was reconsidering plans for Gateway Labs, its London-based hub that provides support for biotech startups.

The UK Government has struggled to revive overseas interest in Britain in recent years, with the number of foreign direct investment projects falling 12pc last year to a record low of 1,375, according to official figures.

High energy costs, recent pressures like the increase in employers’ National Insurance contributions and wider geopolitical uncertainty have all been cited as reasons for declining investment in the UK.

One of the key concerns of Britain’s pharmaceuticals industry has been over NHS drug pricing in the UK. 

After postponing its investment, Dave Ricks, the chief executive of Eli Lilly, said the UK was “probably the worst country in Europe” for drug prices.

The Government agreed to a deal with the US at the start of the month to scrap tariffs on UK drug shipments – in a bid to end the row with Britain’s pharmaceutical industry. 

Dame Emma welcomed the deal as “a step in the right direction” for Britain.

The agreement means the UK will pay more for medicines through the NHS, in return for a guarantee that US import taxes on pharmaceuticals made in Britain will remain at zero for three years.

data.title: BP’s use of Chinese steel is a betrayal of British manufacturing
data.description:
Steelmaking is on its knees in this country, of that there can be no doubt.

British Steel has been under the control of a government-appointed receiver since last April, after ministers were forced to step in and save it from bankruptcy.

As the first anniversary of that bailout approaches, there is no word on when or how it will be returned to private ownership despite the bill to taxpayers rapidly approaching the £300m mark.

The same fate has befallen industrialist Sanjeev Gupta. Once nicknamed the “saviour of steel”, his Speciality Steels UK business collapsed into compulsory liquidation last summer, prompting a scramble in Whitehall to find a buyer.

Yet five months later, Britain’s third-biggest steel manufacturer remains in state hands.

In Port Talbot, things are even more grim.

Traditional steelmaking at the South Wales site vanished in 2024 after more than 100 years. Three thousand people lost their jobs and the effect on the surrounding communities is widely considered to have been devastating.

Armed with a £500m taxpayer-funded grant, its Indian owners Tata planned to turn it into a hub for so-called “green steel”.

With the future of such a vital sector never more bleak, a government announcement in the autumn that British-made steel should be prioritised in big UK manufacturing contracts was a highly welcome lifeline.

What a shame, then, that BP, a genuine titan of British industry, together with its partners on a multibillion-pound energy project in Teesside, apparently didn’t get the memo.

The decision to use Chinese steel to help build Net Zero Teesside – a £4bn green power plant – has rightly prompted outrage, as well as demands for answers from bewildered ministers.

Whichever way you cut it, there is no reasonable excuse for handing a Chinese steelmaker a multimillion-pound order when British Steel has said it is capable of providing the materials.

No wonder that Chris McDonald, the industry minister, has said he will be meeting BP “urgently” to discuss this.

It’s bad enough that this is happening on the site where the Redcar steelworks once stood before it too had to close its doors for good.

As Lord Houchen, the Tees Valley Mayor, rightly said, it is a “betrayal” of British workers, British manufacturing, and those who lost their jobs when the Redcar site had to be mothballed.

This is doubly so given one of the reasons for the disappearance of the Teesside plant was that it was unable to contend with a steep slump in steel prices triggered by a tsunami of state-subsidised Chinese imports.

But that’s not even the worst of it.

What makes this such an egregious snub is that Net Zero Teesside – or NZT as it is imaginatively known – is one of the largest taxpayer-funded projects being built today.

Set to be the world’s first gas-powered electricity plant with its own carbon capture and storage system, and capable of powering around one million homes, support for the project was unveiled to almost farcical levels of fanfare back in 2024.

Even Sir Keir Starmer joined in on the act. Here was proof that Labour was investing in our “industrial heartlands” after being “held back by 14 years of instability and low growth”, the Prime Minister proclaimed.

What’s more, this magical “home-grown energy” would also mean “more money in working people’s pockets”, while simultaneously protecting billpayers “so tyrants like Putin can’t attack the living standards of working people again.”

Phew. No wonder the Government is throwing money at this thing to make sure it gets off the ground.

Not only has the plant been the beneficiary of billions of pounds in government grants, finance, and loan guarantees to help bankroll its construction – the site will also receive massive state subsidies once it’s up and running.

Through a so-called Dispatchable Power Agreement, the Government guarantees the plant a minimum income for 15 years, even if electricity prices drop.

This cosy arrangement is similar to the Contracts for Difference regime through which Ed Miliband has thrown untold billions at wind power providers as part of his green crusade.

Yet despite being handed what some might consider equally lavish sums to bankroll the development of unproven carbon capture technology, BP and its fellow backers – Equinor and TotalEnergies – have awarded China’s Modern Modular Engineering and Construction Company a £5m contract to provide seven million tonnes of steel.

The move becomes even more infuriating when you consider that the same type of steel is available from domestic suppliers including British Steel, a company that British taxpayers have also been propping up – to the tune of £1m a day for the last nine and a half months.

It’s certainly not the sort of joined-up thinking that would form the basis of a coherent industrial strategy.

In the summer, there was much excitement after the Government unveiled plans for new public procurement rules that would require major infrastructure projects to use UK materials including steel.

This isn’t state overreach as some devoted free marketers might claim. It’s just plain common sense.

Yes, UK-made steel might be more costly than its Chinese equivalent, but any premium will surely be recovered from the ripple effect that comes from job creation and regional growth alone.

Why would you allow China, a country that has done more to undermine British industry than any other, to benefit at the expense of the UK economy? It’s pure self-harm.

But as BP has demonstrated, such stipulations count for little if they’re not compulsory.

Nor does it say much for all the excitable promises that have been made about how the millions of jobs destroyed through de-industrialisation will be replaced by even more green ones.

On the contrary, the folks up in Teesside claim that the reason a Chinese supplier was chosen over British Steel is because it comes from environmentally friendly electric arc furnaces, as opposed to the dirty blast furnaces up in Scunthorpe.

Once again, it is hard to escape the conclusion that the benefits of Labour’s clean power crusade have been wildly overstated.

As for BP, it should reverse its decision immediately and show that the “British” in British Petroleum still counts for something.

data.title: ‘Un-pensioning’ could set a very dangerous precedent indeed
data.description:
Join Michael in the comment section below from 11am.

At least 150 teachers have been banned from schools over the last 10 years for child sexual abuse.

The true figure could be considerably higher as Freedom of Information Act requests show that since 2015 a further 256 were struck off for non-defined sexual misconduct and another 245 for “breach of boundaries/trust”.

Yet all these nearly 700 former teachers – indeed all 1,095 teachers who have been disbarred for whatever reason since 2015 – have retained whatever pension entitlements they previously accrued.

The power exists to strip teachers of their pension if they commit treason (thankfully still not the most common of occurrences), serious violations of the Official Secrets Act (there will be little in the way of classified information lying around most schools) or offences committed in connection with their role that could damage confidence in the profession or are “gravely injurious to the state”.

But this power has not been exercised in recent decades. No teacher has lost their pension since at least 2005. The power is also rarely used in relation to others in the public sector – under the NHS pension scheme just 33 people have been stripped of their entitlements since the 1970s.

Now, victims groups are calling for this to change and for those who have so despicably breached the trust placed in them to lose their pensions.

One must have sympathy with this line of argument.

Few sane people would wish to stand up for the rights of paedophiles. Those who abuse children in their care fully deserve to be punished to the full extent the law permits.

There is a very strong case for awarding the pensions of abusers to their victims. Such actions are not just criminal, they are also civil wrongs for which victims are entitled to sue for compensation and seize assets from their abusers.

But in terms of the state stripping people of their pension entitlements, let us be careful what we wish for.

This is a dangerous road to go down, especially in the hands of our current Labour Government and the woke educational and NHS blob. What starts with a clampdown on sex abusers could soon end up with public sector workers losing their pensions for wrong-speak. The legions of the debanked – almost half a million customers last year – could then be joined by the unpensioned.

This is not an argument about the sustainability of public sector pensions and whether the current arrangements can be justified. The Teachers’ Pension Scheme costs taxpayers around £9bn per year. The total liability of public sector pensions is estimated at £5.8tn.

If all these schemes were closed now, annual costs would still rise for decades – peaking at £132bn in 2061.

The whole system is in urgent need of reform, but that is a completely different issue from individuals losing their entitlements for misconduct. In addition, that public sector pensions are such a costly behemoth is not the fault of individual teachers, nurses or doctors. It is a consequence of decades of cowardice by politicians of all parties, finding it easier to avoid having to take difficult decisions.

The reason why unpensioning miscreants is generally wrong, I would argue, is that don’t imagine if ministers start to get a taste for such action they will stop with such offenders. Again and again, policies which have started as a means of combatting a genuine issue have morphed into a much wider bureaucratic power grab.

Just take a look at the Prevent programme at schools, set up to combat Islamist extremism. Examining the scheme now, you might imagine that the biggest domestic threat to British national security did not come from Islamic State adherents and their fellow travellers, but from the extreme Right.

In the year to the end of March 2024, 45pc of referrals were for “extreme Right concerns”, almost double the 23pc referred for “Islamist concerns”. Another 18pc were referred for the rather catch all-sounding “conflicted ideology”.

The case against unpensioning people we wholly disapprove of is the same as why we should champion free speech for those with views we abhor.

Standing up for the rights of people we broadly agree with to express their views is simple. The real test is whether we are willing to stand up for the rights of those whose views we hate.

It is the easiest thing in the world for someone of my world view to be outraged if a university bans a speaker for their Brexit-y, gender critical or pro-Israel views. It is much more difficult – and for that reason all the more important – to stand up for freedom of expression for trans activists and those taking, say, a virulent anti-Israel stance.

If we clamour for the unpensioning of those who are genuinely reprehensible, we will have handed another tool to the Whitehall machine to regulate behaviour they don’t approve of. And who knows who will be next in their sights.

By past performance, it could well be those expressing views which run counter to the prevailing consensus. And those views will generally be of a conservative persuasion.

data.title: US ramps up pressure on UK to abandon net zero
data.description:
Britain and Europe should reverse their net zero policies and buy more American gas, Donald Trump’s energy secretary has said.

Chris Wright said Britain’s push to ramp up renewables and shut down fossil fuels was driving up energy costs and sending British industry offshore.

He also signalled the next front in the US president’s pressure campaign on Europe, criticising the EU’s sustainability directive, its rules on methane emissions and its tax on imported carbon.

Speaking at the World Economic Forum (WEF) in Davos, Switzerland, he said: “That is making it harder and riskier for businesses from the US to bring energy into Europe.

“[The EU] is essentially regulating businesses’ activities all around the world, just if you want to bring a product into Europe.”

Mr Wright added: “They can levy huge fines. What that means to an American business is – if those regulations are not meaningfully reformed – it’s not worth it for them to send the energy into Europe.”

He warned Britain and Brussels that if they did not change course, they could remain dependent on Russian gas or on high-carbon alternatives from Asia.

Mr Wright’s salvo came a day after Mr Trump used his speech at Davos to rail against “the green new scam”.

The president berated Britain for failing to exploit oil and gas reserves in the North Sea and said net zero policies were stifling power generation and driving up electricity prices. He called buyers of Chinese-made wind turbines “stupid people”.

Mr Trump’s tirade caused division in Davos. Climate and sustainability have often been central to the WEF agenda – including a star turn by Greta Thunberg, the climate campaigner, in 2020.

“At the World Economic Forum, far more attention was paid to risks from climate than to other risks for many years,” said Sir Niall Ferguson, the historian and Hoover Institution fellow. “Not that there aren’t risks from climate change – but they’re not as clear and present a danger as the risk from, say, Vladimir Putin.”

Others pushed back against Mr Trump’s stance. Andrew Forrest, the mining billionaire and green energy developer, told The Telegraph that if renewable energy was stupid, he was “happy to be called stupid”.

Hamad Hussain, a Capital Economics analyst, said the White House could potentially “weaponise” America’s growing liquefied natural gas (LNG) trade with Europe.

“Strains in US-Europe relations under president Trump mean that European policymakers may perceive the region’s growing dependence on the US for energy to be a strategic responsibility,” he said in a note.

Mr Wright said the UK and EU had “increased dependence on oil and natural gas while producing less of them”.

Abandoning coal had come at a “huge societal cost” to Europe, whereas Mr Trump was reversing the kind of “UK energy policies” pursued by Joe Biden, the former US president, he added.

Mr Wright said Mr Trump had prevented the closure of 17 gigawatts of coal-fired power last year. This had helped meet the energy-intensive demands of the AI-led boom in data centres.

He added: “Most of these coal plants were ordered to close, just like in the UK, for political reasons … Forget the rate payers, forget the risk of blackouts, forget the closing off of opportunity for new data centres or reshoring of manufacturing.

“We’ve seen a pretty large-scale deindustrialisation of Germany, of the United Kingdom, of Europe as a whole and obviously an over-dependence on Russia.”

This could even be increasing global greenhouse gas emissions, Mr Wright said. This was because industries would be pushed out to countries in Asia that still used coal-fired power plants, with the products then coming back to Europe on diesel-powered ships.

He said: “I wouldn’t call that a green energy transition, I just call that a movement of industry.”

Mr Wright said he was hopeful the White House could strike a deal with Brussels: “I think in our dialogues with European regulators, everyone understands the issue and I think practically wants to fix it.

“It’s not fixed yet but I think there’s strong will on both sides to make it so that we can have an easier flow of trade.”

data.title: How Britain banned beauty
data.description:
What we know today as the classical Georgian terrace emerged from a catastrophe.

In the wake of the Great Fire of London in 1666, Parliament passed a series of acts to change the way houses were designed. 

This meant the capital went from having narrow, cramped streets full of overhanging Tudor homes – complete with wooden beams and eaves – to more uniform, elegant and brick-built terraces. 

This triggered what is regarded as Britain’s first speculative building boom, with developers throwing up streets of houses using new construction techniques.

Hundreds of years later, Georgian homes are still beloved by most Britons with polls consistently showing they are actually preferred over new housing.

An Ipsos Mori survey in 2015 found Georgian-style homes were the most popular, preferred by 75pc, while research by Anglian Home Improvements in 2019 found that Georgian architecture was Britain’s “most desirable” property style.

In 2023, research by the Chartered Institute of Building also concluded that most buyers viewed older homes as higher quality. Famous neighbourhoods known for their Georgian homes, such as Clifton in Bristol and Bedford Square in London, are among the country’s most sought-after locations. 

Whereas modern developments can feel lacking in character, Georgian properties are known for their ornamental features: pediments and cornicing, pillars and panelled walls, attractive quoining and wrought iron railings.

“Georgian terraced homes are light-filled, they feel natural, they've got pattern, variety and a little bit of joy,” says Nicholas Boys-Smith, of design consultancy Create Streets.

“They might have a fan window, a bit of texture, but they’ve also got a dignity and a sort of symmetry and order to them. 

“It's a good compromise between our brain’s need for order and our love of ornament and detail and playfulness.”

It’s a far cry from what many people find today when they tour one of Britain’s modern housing estates. 

Home buyers are often faced with streets of drab and unappealing new-builds. 

We can all picture these homes in our minds. They are carbon copies of each other, scattered across sprawling new neighbourhoods. The Chartered Institute of Building’s 2023 survey found two fifths of the public believed modern designs suffered from a “lack of character”.

“Surely, part of the function of a building is not just to provide shelter to those who live or use it internally, but also to bring joy and dignity to the much greater number of people who are going to pass by it,” says Boys-Smith. 

“So having a boxy and ugly building that is discourteous to the landscape and careless of a local tradition is thus actually not performing part of its function for the people who live nearby.”

Politicians from David Cameron to Sir Keir Starmer have repeatedly promised to make new developments more attractive. 

Some, including former housing secretary Michael Gove, have even argued that making homes beautiful is the antidote to Nimbyism. 

Yet instead, things seem to be getting worse. For instance, large windows are the most sought-after feature for house-hunters, according to Anglian Homes Improvement, but they keep getting smaller. Why?

One answer, according to experts, is that it is now all but impossible to build the kind of classical homes many of us love.

Through layer upon layer of well-meaning red tape, Britain has effectively banned beauty.

In 2006, then opposition leader David Cameron gave a speech criticising the way new developments looked and vowed to provide “homes that are beautiful in communities that are sustainable”.

However, 17 years later – and 13 years into government – the Tories were still railing against the standard of housing being delivered, with Michael Gove warning it was too often “ugly or identikit”. 

Clearly, no politician ever set out to encourage the building of eyesores across Britain. 

Yet according to experts, that is what successive governments have accidentally done with a blizzard of regulations.

Take windows, for example. Under rules first introduced under the Major government, upstairs windows have to be either 1.1 metres from the floor or guarded so people can't fall out of them. 

Just how necessary this protection is may be open to debate. The Government does not publish up-to-date figures on how many people die from falling out of windows, but the number is thought to be very small. 

Figures from the Office for National Statistics suggest an average of 77 people died per year after falling from windows, balconies, roofs, and walls from 2015 to 2024. However, this included the likes of builders who work at height.

Thirteen children died from falling out of windows between April 2019 and May 2025, according to Bristol University's National Child Mortality Database.

Many developers overcame these window safety rules by restricting how far they could open. But in 2022, then-housing secretary Michael Gove introduced rules that said windows must also open far enough to ventilate the room with fresh air during hot weather. This effectively ruled out window restrictors and meant developers switched to using smaller, squatter windows to comply with both rules.

Some get around this by putting bars over the windows, costing more than £200 per window, which many homeowners later remove anyway. 

Ironically, the regulations mean sash windows – famously good at creating draughts with their top and bottom openings – cannot be included in new builds in certain areas such as London which are deemed to be at “higher risk of overheating”. Getting them might be possible through carrying out expensive heat dynamics surveys, but many developers don’t want to do this. 

Samuel Hughes, of the Centre for Policy Studies, says the rules are a particularly bad example of “paternalistic” overreach by the Government. 

“It's amazing to me that building safety regulators think it's their job to tell British people what size of windows they ought to want,” he says. 

“People know how windows work.”

Meanwhile, other regulations dictate the proportions of homes in ways that rule out classical Georgian styles. 

Rules updated in 2004 and 2015 say all new homes must have step-free access, a lavatory suitable for wheelchairs on the ground floor and wider doors and hallways.

They are designed to make homes more accessible to people with disabilities – but tend to rule out popular features such as steps to the front door, while preventing the narrower, more space-efficient layouts of Georgian terraces. 

Minimum back-to-back distances between properties also mean houses must be spaced further apart. This results in developers being able to build fewer homes than before on the same patch of land. While Georgian homes can span densities of anywhere between 40 to 175 homes per hectare, most modern developments will only have 20 to 30 per hectare, according to Create Streets. 

That is partly because Georgian-style properties may lack driveways or have smaller gardens. Take these two examples: The city street with Georgian homes has twice as many homes as the rural new build development. 

Despite these drawbacks on driveways and gardens, Boys-Smith of Create Streets argues that such homes are nicer places to live overall.

“The great advantage of slightly higher density is that you just don't need to build on as much farmland to provide the homes we need,” he says.

“But also, you create a neighbourhood which is actually more sustainable because it’s easier to walk and cycle more if the distance to the nearest shop or pub or school is smaller.”

There is nothing inevitable about the kind of housing Britain has ended up with. Look internationally and you can find modern examples of classical-style housing that prioritises attractiveness.

In the Parisian suburb of Clamart, for example, a regeneration project over the last decade has seen blocks of flats built in the instantly recognisable 19th century Haussmann style, with architects central to the project.

As the Government prepares to build 12 new towns across England, beauty risks being overlooked. But Boys-Smith and others believe there is a modern template worth studying: Poundbury.

A traditionalist “urban extension” to Dorchester, Poundbury was the King’s passion project – intended as a rebuke to modernist architecture. 

There, designers found clever ways to incorporate traditional Georgian styles into modern homes – even within current planning rules and regulations. 

For example, many homes in Poundbury still feature sash windows. Designers complied with the regulations by making sure they only opened from the top and in some cases by installing bars.

A large number of houses also have steps to the front door, despite accessibility regulations. This is because side or back doors have been made step-free instead.

When he unveiled the new towns programme last year, Sir Keir Starmer hailed Poundbury as an example of the “gentle density” the Government wants to emulate in future developments. 

It’s a formula that has proved popular – and rewarding – for local residents. 

According to Create Streets, homes in Poundbury will sell at 55pc more value per hectare of development than other areas. Researchers said this is because people are willing to pay more to live in such properties.

Blake Holt, a local resident who moved to Poundbury from the countryside, says more developers should follow the scheme’s example.

The 78-year-old grandfather and his wife love how walkable the development is, as well as its varied and interesting architecture. 

“Having seen some other more recent estates that are being built, they look awful. They’re boxy and don’t feel like attractive places to live,” he says.

“In Poundbury, we love the architecture. It’s the way everything fits together. Everything is designed in a way that makes for much more attractive streetscapes.

“I think developers have a lot to learn from it.”

The Government insisted that building regulations do not completely ban features such as sash windows but admitted they do preclude them in places such as London where properties are “at higher risk of overheating”.

A spokesman for the ministry of housing and local government said: “Our landmark new towns will be attractive, well designed places where people want to live. 

“There is no ban on sash windows – or any type of window – and just today we have launched new design guidance to make sure we’re building homes and neighbourhoods people want to live in.

“This Government is taking action to fix the housing crisis we inherited and build 1.5 million homes to restore the dream of home ownership.”

Beauty isn’t completely banned yet. But unless Britain rediscovers its classical flair on a much grander scale, it may as well be.

data.title: Wind farm backers complain over plan to squeeze subsidies
data.description:
Wind and solar developers have criticised the Government’s decision to squeeze subsidies by shifting their payments to a lower inflation measure.

Ministers have triggered a row by switching the Renewables Obligation (RO) scheme from the retail price index (RPI) to the consumer price index (CPI).

The move is designed to save the Government up to £270m a year by 2030, as officials try to combat the spiralling cost of net zero subsidies.

However, the change has frustrated some of the green investors working to help the UK achieve its target of generating almost entirely clean power by 2030.

Ross Grier, chief investment officer at NextEnergy, one of Britain’s leading renewables investors, said he was disappointed that “the Government is taking steps that are expected to harm investor confidence at a time when we need more capital than ever to deliver the energy transition in a timely fashion”.

Steven Agnew, head of policy at wind industry trade body Renewable UK, added: “Our industry is delivering investment in energy infrastructure.

“Today’s announcement of changes to inflation indexation could undermine our efforts by raising the cost of capital.”

Figures show that the RO, which offers green energy developers guaranteed subsidies for two decades, currently adds £8bn a year to energy bills.

The RO scheme is closed to new applicants, although some green energy developers are expected to keep receiving yearly subsidies until 2037, rising with inflation.

After announcing the change, Government officials said that “CPI indexation will continue to offer sufficient inflation protection for RO assets”.

But investors said that retrospective changes to existing subsidies would deter future investment.

Research by the Renewable Energy Foundation showed that the RO added £67bn to bills between 2002 and 2024.

The scheme currently adds about £110 to the average annual domestic power bill of £963, making it the most expensive renewable subsidy to date.

Vestas cuts hundreds of jobs

Changes to the RO scheme emerged as a wind turbine factory on the Isle of Wight confirmed the loss of 300 jobs on Wednesday, despite being handed a £20m grant from Ed Miliband.

Danish manufacturer Vestas said it has halved its workforce but claimed government funding would safeguard the remaining 300 jobs on the site.

The job losses have been confirmed after Vestas previously cast doubt over the future of the factory, which makes blades for offshore turbines.

The company had indicated in 2024 that the site was uneconomic, undermining the Energy Secretary’s attempts to turn Britain into a clean energy superpower.

Vestas has said the £20m will be used to convert the factory to produce blades for onshore wind farms, rather than focusing solely on offshore.

Katie White, the climate minister, said: “It was a no-brainer to save the Vestas factory on the Isle of Wight and create the country’s only dedicated onshore wind blade facility.

“While our thoughts are with those who have lost their jobs, by safeguarding over 300 roles, we are protecting one of the Isle of Wight’s largest employers and giving Britain the ability to produce the homegrown, clean power we need to bring bills down for good.”

However, the deal is likely to raise questions over why taxpayer funding is needed to support a company at the heart of the renewables industry, supposedly one of the UK’s fastest-growing sectors.

A spokesman for the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero said it was securing the plant’s future after a period of uncertainty.

“Logistical constraints on the site meant the Danish manufacturer could not produce the next generation of larger offshore blades,” they said.

data.title: BBC shuts down investigations team in battle to slash costs
data.description:
The BBC has shut down a team dedicated to rooting out online fraud as part of cost-cutting efforts.

BBC Trending, which was tasked with investigating fake and fraudulent material on social media, will close despite the broadcaster’s attempts to counter disinformation online.

The move is expected to result in around four job losses, with affected staff informed of the cuts late last year.

BBC Trending was launched in 2013 by Mukul Devichand, now a senior editor at the New York Times. Dubbed the BBC’s “bureau on the internet”, it published online investigations and produced a weekly radio show on the World Service.

Recent stories included an investigation into a network of fraudsters profiting from fake AI-generated images of Holocaust victims, as well as exposing an elaborate scam in which criminals posed as Chinese police officers.

The decision to shut down the team, first reported by industry magazine Deadline, comes amid broader cost-cutting at the BBC as it attempts to plug a £500m black hole in its finances caused by a drop in licence fee revenues.

The public service broadcaster last year said it would cut 130 jobs from the World Service in an effort to save £6m.

That followed broader cuts across BBC News, including the closure of programmes such as the Hardtalk interview show.

Growing concerns over misinformation

However, the cutbacks will raise concerns about the BBC’s ability to counter a surge of misinformation online.

The corporation has cited this as a key factor behind its recent deal to make shows specifically for YouTube.

It said a range of new channels and live streams on the platform would provide “news that cuts through the noise”.

A source close to the BBC insisted that fighting disinformation remained an “absolutely critical” part of the broadcaster’s role. They pointed to recent investment in BBC Verify, its flagship fact-checking unit.

However, the BBC has itself been accused of spreading misinformation through its output.

A leaked whistleblowing report obtained by The Telegraph found that BBC Verify was forced to withdraw a story that incorrectly claimed car insurance companies were racist.

That was part of a broader catalogue of complaints about the BBC’s coverage, including a Panorama episode that misleadingly edited Donald Trump’s speech during the Jan 6 Capitol riots.

The scandal prompted the resignation of both Tim Davie, the director-general, and Deborah Turness, the head of news, while the US president has filed a $10bn (£7.3bn) defamation lawsuit against the BBC.

The closure comes as the BBC enters critical negotiations with ministers over the future of the licence fee ahead of the end of the current Charter period next year.

Lisa Nandy, the Culture Secretary, has said she is considering a range of possible reforms including funding the broadcaster through advertising or subscriptions.

In an interview with The Guardian this week, Mr Davie acknowledged that the BBC faced “profound jeopardy” unless it embraced an overhaul of its funding model. 

However, he dismissed both advertising and subscriptions, arguing that the BBC should remain a universal service.

data.title: Pensioner unable to call ambulance because BT landline cut
data.description:
A pensioner who suffered a stroke was unable to call an ambulance when their BT landline stopped working.

Graham Chapman, 75, from Horsehay in Shropshire, fell ill earlier this month. When his wife Jan went to call for help, she realised the phone was “completely dead”, the BBC reported.

The couple’s digital landline had been rendered useless after an error in upgrading their broadband.

The telecom giant is one of many rolling out “Digital Voice”, a system that has scrapped traditional copper landlines and made all phone lines reliant on broadband routers.

Mr and Mrs Chapman had already switched over to the Digital Voice system, which was working prior to their broadband order being cancelled.

When the plans were announced, they provoked concern that the elderly might be left isolated from emergency services, leading BT and Virgin Media O2 to pause deployment in 2023.

An overhaul of the landline system was planned to be completed by the end of 2025, but the deadline has been pushed back to 2027.

Engineers working on Mr Chapman’s home had been unable to connect them to fibre broadband on Jan 12 because they “hadn’t got enough cable”, but assured the pair “everything was working” fine when they left.

Later that week, Mr Chapman suffered a stroke. Mrs Chapman recalled going straight to the landline to ring 999.

“I felt really stressed out”, Mrs Chapman said, describing how she “lifted the phone and it was completely dead. I thought at the time, and I still do, that it’s the worst thing that’s happened to me.”

Fortunately, Mr Chapman was able to receive hospital care after a mobile phone call. He spent a week at Telford’s Princess Royal Hospital and is now recovering at home.

Ofcom has said it could investigate and fine BT if bungled landline cut-offs put customers at risk, after fining Virgin Media £23.8m in 2025 for its handling of the switch-over, saying it had put vulnerable customers at “direct risk of harm”.

The company admitted disconnecting customers who relied on life-saving “telecare” panic buttons to call for medical help.

Recovering from his medical emergency, Mr Chapman called the incident “absolutely scandalous” and said he could “foresee deaths occurring because of it”.

He claimed he had spent more than two weeks trying to have the landline fixed – a process he described as “absolutely terrible, frustrating” and as having affected his recovery.

A BT spokesman confirmed that the Chapmans’ landline has been restored.

They said: “We’re very sorry that the Chapmans’ landline phone was not working, following a broadband upgrade order. We have restored their landline service and confirmed that all is working.

The Telegraph previously revealed that changes to Digital Voice landlines had left a number of vulnerable customers without access to a phone line over Christmas.

Ofcom has said it could investigate and fine BT if bungled landline cut-offs linked to the rollout put customers at risk.

They fined Virgin Media £23.8m in 2025 for its handling of the switch-over, saying it had put vulnerable customers at “direct risk of harm”.

Anna Sabine, a Lib Dem MP and member of the Digital Communities all-party parliamentary group, previously told The Telegraph: “We warned that the safety net wasn’t there, and it is unacceptable that retirees and families in remote areas have paid the price for this botched rollout during the most isolating time of the year.”

A report published by the group in March 2025 warned that the switch-over could have “deeply personal repercussions” for vulnerable older and disabled people.

Chris Bryant, the telecoms minister, said in 2024: “This has kept me up at night and [is] a priority that I have put at the forefront of my work since stepping into office.

“I am pleased telecoms companies, central government, and local authorities are working in lockstep to achieve customer safety.”

A BT spokesman said the firm had been raising awareness of the switch-over for the past two years and that it had reviewed cases reported by The Telegraph earlier this month of vulnerable customers being cut off.

The spokesman added: “We’re very sorry that the Chapmans’ landline phone was not working, following a broadband upgrade order. We have restored their landline service and confirmed that all is working.

“The Chapmans will receive automatic compensation for the period they were unable to use their landline, and their complaint has been resolved.”

data.title: CityFibre to cut hundreds of jobs as debt pressures mount
data.description:
CityFibre is to cut hundreds of jobs as the debt-laden broadband firm braces for a wave of consolidation across the industry.

The British telecoms group, which is backed by Goldman Sachs and Abu Dhabi’s Mubadala fund, on Wednesday outlined plans to slash 450 roles, almost a third of its 1,400-strong workforce.

The company said the redundancies will predominantly affect engineers as it shifts focus from building a full-fibre network to buying up struggling rivals.

In a note to staff, Simon Holden, the chief executive, said CityFibre’s current structure was “no longer the right fit for where our business is heading” and sweeping cuts were needed.

He added: “This is not something we take lightly, and we are committed to working through it together. We believe these changes are necessary to ensure CityFibre continues to deliver for customers while remaining resilient and well-positioned for the future.”

The redundancies, which were first reported by the Financial Times, are the latest sign of cost-cutting in the challenger broadband market.

A host of firms dubbed “alt-nets” sprung up to rival BT, borrowing heavily to fund the construction of expensive full-fibre networks. However, surging interest costs and sluggish take-up have left many struggling to survive.

CityFibre, which has around 850,000 customers, last week said revenues hit a record £170m last year. Its performance has been boosted by a deal to host Sky customers on its network.

But the company omitted key details on the eye-watering cost of servicing its debts, which stand at almost £4bn.

Last year, the telecoms group secured a crucial £2.3bn refinancing following a lengthy stand-off between shareholders and lenders about who would pump in the money. It has said that the funding will last until the middle of next year.

CityFibre has positioned itself as the key challenger to BT’s Openreach and Virgin Media O2 and is now considered a likely suitor to sweep up an array of smaller alt-nets.

One of those, G.Network, recently collapsed into administration after being taken over by private equity firm FitzWalter Capital.

Gigaclear, a rural broadband provider, is also poised to be taken over by lenders, including NatWest and the taxpayer-backed National Wealth Fund.

A CityFibre spokesman said: “As we shift our strategy from building to acquiring networks, backed by our recent £2.3bn financing, the time is right to review how we operate so we are best placed to continue our success through this next phase of growth.

“All of our people have helped to get CityFibre into a very strong position and we will support our teams as we take this step and ensure CityFibre becomes the long-term, sustainable competitor the UK needs.”

data.title: Banks target top 10pc of earners with best mortgage deals
data.description:
Banks have eased mortgage lending to allow buyers to borrow more, with the best deals reserved for higher earners.

Lenders targeting “Henrys” – which stands for “High Earner, Not Rich Yet” – have increased how many times their salary buyers can borrow.

NatWest and Nationwide announced these borrowers would be able to take mortgages of up to six times their salary. 

But the caveat was that single borrowers would need to earn at least £75,000, and couples £100,000, in order to benefit from the more generous lending. For a buyer earning £75,000, putting them in the top 10pc of earners, this would mean being able to borrow an extra £37,500, NatWest said.

The average UK salary was just over £39,000 in April last year, according to the Office for National Statistics (ONS).

City regulators the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) and Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA) relaxed rules about riskier lending last year, allowing borrowers with high incomes to take on larger mortgages.

Traditionally, banks have been hesitant to allow buyers to borrow more than 4.5 times their income – and larger lenders can still only offer 15pc of their mortgages at higher levels.

Henry Jordan, of Nationwide, said: “The government and regulatory changes last year have been a game changer for first-time buyers. Over the past year, we’ve seen a five-fold increase in the number of first-time buyers borrowing between 5.5 and six times income.”

Competition among lenders is expected to continue throughout the year, as lower inflation predictions mean that further cuts to the Bank Rate are expected. Mortgage rates fell over the course of last year as the Bank Rate came down, with the average two-year fixed rate dipping below 5pc for the first time since 2022 last August.

Nottingham Building Society announced earlier this month that it was cutting the earning requirements for its higher multiple mortgages, allowing those earning £65,000 a year to borrow up to 5.5 times their income.

Lender Santander allows those earning between £45,000 and £99,999 to borrow up to five times their income if they have a deposit of at least 10pc. But those earning more than £100,000 a year can borrow 5.5 times their income.

The bank will also allow high earners to have more generous interest-only mortgages. Those earning more than £200,000 can borrow up to 75pc of the value of their home on an interest-only basis – compared to just 50pc for those earning under that threshold.

HSBC now allows its Premier customers to borrow up to 6.5 times their salary. This means a borrower earning £100,000 could borrow £650,000, compared to £550,000 previously.

Adrian Anderson, of mortgage brokerage Anderson Harris, said: “Market factors are also helping banks to lend more. We have noticed as interest rates and mortgage rates have been reducing, the banks’ affordability stress testing has been easing.”

data.title: Britain in danger of outsourcing its intellect, warns AI pioneer
data.description:
The artificial intelligence revolution threatens to erode Britain’s sovereignty, one of the country’s most experienced tech entrepreneurs has warned.

Ken Mulvany, co-founder and executive chairman of Benevolent AI, which has pioneered artificial intelligence in drug discovery, told The Telegraph that the United States and China are increasingly dominant in the crucial field.

He says: “If you look at this from the UK or Europe’s perspective, the risk is that these – China and America – are now the two cognitive powers. They’re the ones that are investing the most into this.

“I do worry about having the nation’s intellect effectively outsourced. It’s not just that, either. It’s the productivity gains.

“If for every hour of cognitive work that you do there’s a machine doing what would take you 50 hours, how do you as a nation compete with that?

“Electricity multiplied the power of muscles and AI can do the same with brains in certain scenarios. But it’s almost like you, as Britain and Europe are outsourcing national IQ to US and Chinese tech companies.

“So you’re left trying to regulate something you don’t own. And I do worry about that. I think of it as cognitive border control.”

Mulvany is better placed than most to sound the alarm. An eerily youthful 57 who betrays his Hawaiian roots with a surf-ready hairdo, he is an entrepreneur who has seen tech boom and bust more than once.

In a world increasingly haunted by visions of an AI apocalypse that would make the dotcom crash look like a minor market setback, Mulvany and Benevolent have already been tested to near-destruction.

The technology excited investors – including the star fund manager Neil Woodford long before his self-inflicted implosion – enough to snag a £1.5bn valuation way back in 2018.

This was long before the likes of Sam Altman of OpenAI and Dario Amodei of Anthropic emerged to seek thrones as the next generation of tech plutocrats.

Benevolent sought to help researchers to quickly design new drugs with computer assistance, using machine learning techniques to scour a treasure trove of science information for possibilities at an unprecedented clip. Expansion into defence and energy markets promised new horizons and riches.

It seemed to work. Benevolent burst onto the public markets long before the current AI frontrunners had attracted the attention of everyday investors, too. With the world still in the grip of the pandemic and having discovered an anti-inflammatory that was proving useful on pandemic wards, the start-up made a splashy debut.

“I wasn’t really part of it at that time,” Mulvany recalls ruefully. Having seemingly set up Benevolent for enormous success, he was already easing into semi-retirement.

As it secured a lucrative listing in Amsterdam, Benevolent won endorsement and investment from some of the pharmaceutical industry’s biggest names. AstraZeneca backed Benevolent as part of a transaction that raised hundreds of millions.

Alas, Mulvany’s big payday and exit did not set the scene for a sustainable company. A starry board and big executive hires steered Benevolent off a cliff.

The company lost almost all its value within two years, prompting Mulvany to abandon his early retirement and mount a boardroom coup to take back control last year. He had become frustrated that Benevolent had shifted from his concept of a technology company to a drug developer in its own right, with all the risk and costs associated with it.

“They brought in some luminaries,” Mulvany recalls. “Some really heavy hitters from pharma. And Benevolent morphed from a tech company to a pharma company. For me as an investor, and for some of my fellow investors, we didn’t have the appetite for that. We were technology investors.

“You had a technology shareholder base and a management and board doing biotech. All of its revenue was from its technology but it was spending a ton of money developing drugs.

“The technology cost was very low relative to drug development. So I became more of an activist shareholder at that point because it wasn’t going the way I wanted it to go and the markets reflected that too. It lost 95pc of its value.”

It’s a tale of hype meeting with financial and scientific reality that now seems to be playing out on an extreme scale as OpenAI and its rivals test the limits of pre-profit funding and investor patience. Altman alone has committed to spending more than $1tn (£750bn).

Having abandoned his retirement to mount his successful coup, Mulvany is now rebuilding Benevolent as an enterprise software company. Its new software and services are focused sharply on helping drug developers, rather than developing drugs. It’s a more commercial proposition, with a closer eye on returns for Benevolent and its big pharma customers.

“We help on the science side of things, reviewing the literature, but also how you make business decisions as a drug developer, how you expand into a particular market, how you design trials and all of that.

“People in the industry are making decisions with a fraction of the information they need.”

These are still the early days of Benevolent’s attempted comeback and Mulvany’s unexpected return from early retirement, which he had intended to spend skiing. His hopes of using the company’s targeted AI technology beyond drug discovery in fields such as defence and energy have been revived.

Benevolent, without revenue, now has the cash and investor support to pursue its new business into 2027, according to Mulvany.

Despite the mounting fears of an AI bust, he remains a believer in the technology. After a deep cull of staff, Benevolent’s coders are 50 times faster with the help of computer intelligence, for instance. But Mulvany, now an AI survivor, admits a shake-out lies ahead for those with grandiose visions.

“I saw this in the dotcom. I was there right at the bubble. But whether it’s railroads, or electricity or the internet there has always been over-investment and hype.

“So there’s going to be losers in this but the infrastructure is being put in place. So yeah, you’re seeing an AI bubble, but I’ve seen it before.”

data.title: Britain to extend life of ageing nuclear plants to keep the lights on
data.description:
Two of Britain’s oldest nuclear power plants could be kept running for an extra two years because of an acute electricity shortage in the UK.

Hartlepool and Heysham 1, owned by EDF, were due to shut down in 2028, but ministers want to extend the operating licences to at least 2030 because the UK faces “a dangerous gap” in power supplies if they shut.

Both have already been operating for 42 years despite being scheduled to close for safety reasons in 2008.

EDF, France’s state-owned power utility, which operates all five UK nuclear stations, said it was working to keep the stations operational without compromising safety.

Mark Hartley, from EDF, said: “In November, the UK Government said that the retirement of these Advanced Gas-Cooled Reactors (AGRs) risks leaving a dangerous gap in Britain’s low-carbon energy supply.

“It is our ambition to generate from the remaining AGR stations for as long as it is safe and commercially viable to do so, and we will keep their lifetimes under review to assess whether further life extensions can be achieved.”

EDF will invest £1.2bn in the latest extensions, which will keep enough power on the grid to power about four million homes.

That power is vital to Energy Secretary Ed Miliband’s plans to decarbonise the UK’s power supplies by 2030.

Last year, nuclear stations generated 12pc of the UK’s electricity. This was 12pc lower than in 2024 because of an extended outage at Hartlepool power station.

Losing two nuclear stations would leave the UK dependent on imports via subsea interconnectors, but these might not always meet the nation’s needs.

Sizewell B, the UK’s largest nuclear plant, is already due to operate until 2035, and EDF hopes to extend this to 2055.

Two other stations, Torness and Heysham 2, were originally scheduled to close in 2023 and have been cleared to generate until March 2030 after EDF invested £8.6bn in the fleet.

The fate of Heysham 1 and Hartlepool is less certain and will depend on the results of safety assessments.

AGR reactors contain radioactive uranium fuel pellets surrounded by massive graphite blocks that absorb the high-energy neutrons emitted by the fuel, thereby controlling the nuclear reaction.

However, over time, these blocks tend to crack due to the intense radiation and heat to which they are exposed.

Such cracks have already forced the closure of several other UK power stations.

EDF’s safety assessment will need to be ratified by the Office for Nuclear Regulation, which will need to approve the extensions as safe.

It follows a report issued last week by energy consultancy Watt-Logic, warning that Britain risks electricity rationing by the next general election, with the risk peaking in the winter months between 2028 and 2031 due to a loss of energy production.

This is also because many of the UK’s ageing gas-fired power stations are at risk of failure between now and 2030, and because Hinkley Point C, the giant nuclear station under construction in Somerset, is several years behind schedule.

The UK closed its last coal-fired power station, at Ratcliffe in Yorkshire, last year.

Lord Vallance, the nuclear minister, welcomed EDF’s move.

“Nuclear is a vital component of our energy mix, providing clean homegrown power for millions of homes and supporting energy security while employing thousands of people across the country,” he said.
