data.title: Crackdown on water companies ripping up roads after surge in disruption
data.description:
Water and energy companies will face penalties for ripping up London’s streets after a surge in roadworks worsened congestion across the capital.

Under plans put forward by Transport for London (TfL), utility firms will be charged for excavating roads and disrupting traffic during rush hour in a bid to minimise the impact on journey times.

The penalty scheme was previously trialled on London’s busiest roads, but will now be extended across streets in 26 boroughs, according to TfL.

It said: “The scheme charges utility companies and others a fee to dig up the busiest roads at the busiest times, reducing congestion and providing funds for innovative new ways to reduce delays on the roads.

“This has been proven to reduce congestion, improve journeys and enhance air quality.”

The extent of the charges will vary according to the road and will mostly apply during the busiest periods of the day, including between 6.30am and 9.30am, and 3.30pm and 7pm.

Daily fees will typically range from £350 for impeding a footpath to £2,500 for a main road.

Around 2.2 million street works were carried out in England between April 2023 and March 2024, with more than 200 organisations holding the right to dig up roads.

Works performed by utilities on water, gas, energy and broadband networks have increased by 30pc over the past decade, according to the Local Government Association.

Under the latest crackdown, Sir Sadiq Khan, the Mayor of London, will take control of the penalty scheme from the Department for Transport.

Camden, Enfield, Lambeth and Merton have already received approval for their proposals to include more key routes in the scheme, while 22 other boroughs are working on applications.

A study carried out by transport analytics firm Inrix suggests that the number of planned roadworks in London has increased by almost 400pc since 2012, while journey times are lengthening by 12pc annually.

The study named London as Europe’s most congested city in 2024 and said the cost of congestion totalled £3.85bn, or £942 per driver. For the UK as a whole, the figure was £7.7bn, up £200m on the previous year.

‘Horrible cycle of roadworks’

Transport bosses last year complained to MPs that workmen were increasingly abusing emergency powers to dig up roads at little or no notice to avoid submitting more formal plans.

In evidence to the transport select committee, the Confederation of Passenger Transport, which represents bus operators, said its members were experiencing a surge in last-minute roadworks that gave them no time to prepare for lengthy traffic jams.

The AA said motorists were being subjected to a “horrible cycle of roadworks that never seem to end” and said utilities should be compelled to coordinate their activities rather than digging one hole after another in the same place.

TfL’s five-year roadworks plan also proposes the increased use of software that adjusts traffic light timings to reduce delays.

data.title: Royal Mail tycoon takes £320m dividend after earning millions from wasted wind
data.description:
The billionaire owner of Royal Mail has taken a £320m dividend from his UK power business after it earned tens of millions of pounds from wasted wind.

EP UK Investments – which is controlled by Daniel Kretinsky, the Czech tycoon – paid out the sum in 2024, recently published accounts showed. His dividend rose from £50m the previous year.

Mr Kretinsky’s EP UK owns stakes in energy assets including power stations at South Humber Bank and Langage, Devon, as well as the Lynemouth biomass plant in Northumberland. It also acts as an energy trading company, buying and selling contracts for electricity, gas and carbon.

It is one of several energy giants paid to fire up gas power plants when Britain’s wind farms are switched off because of capacity issues.

Analysis for The Telegraph previously revealed that Mr Kretinsky’s company made £92m in 2024 from a practice known as “curtailment”, in which wind turbines are told to stop generating because the grid is too congested to accept their power.

The huge dividend risks reigniting a row over ballooning curtailment costs. Figures released this week showed Britain’s wind farm turbines wasted enough energy to power all of London’s homes last year.

Major suppliers including Octopus Energy have called for the country to be divided into different wholesale markets through a system known as zonal pricing, though experts have warned this would drive up bills for households in the South.

Mr Kretinsky’s £3.6bn takeover of Royal Mail last year was subject to intense political scrutiny and a national security review, owing to concerns about the 500-year-old postal service falling into foreign hands for the first time.

He is also the largest shareholder in Sainsbury’s and holds a 27pc stake in Premier League club West Ham United.

Accounts showed EP UK enjoyed a surge in pre-tax profits in 2024, rising from £99m the previous year to £152m, even as revenues halved to £1.1bn amid falling wholesale electricity prices.

Bosses said the improved profitability was a result of lower energy buying costs as the company reduced its power purchases, while trading commissions also increased.

The £320m dividend was paid to Luxembourg-based parent company EP Investments, which is controlled by Mr Kretinsky. The parent company also issued a £270m loan during the year to fund projects at its subsidiaries.

Mr Kretinsky has enjoyed booming profits from his British power assets in recent years as Russia’s invasion of Ukraine triggered chaos in energy markets.

Gas-fired power generators were exempted from the Government’s windfall tax, which was aimed at capping profits for energy firms as household bills soared. This was because they faced rising fuel costs as wholesale gas prices jumped.

The South Humber Bank and Langage plants have a combined capacity of around 2.3 gigawatts, capable of supplying power to around 1.5 million homes.

In the accounts, bosses said EP UK was continuing to explore further investment and development opportunities in Britain.

The holding company bought a power station in Corby, Northamptonshire, last year and increased its investment in the West Burton Energy power plant, near Gainsborough, Lincolnshire, which it runs as a joint venture with TotalEnergies.

However, Mr Kretinsky’s energy empire also scaled back plans to build a new gas-fired power station at Eggborough, North Yorkshire, in a setback to the back-up system designed to prevent blackouts in Britain.

data.title: Treasury doesn’t understand risks of shadow banking bubble, Lords warn
data.description:
Treasury ministers have come under fire from an influential group of peers for failing to grasp the risks of Wall Street’s shadow banking bubble.

On Thursday, the House of Lords’ financial services regulation committee heavily criticised the Government for having “demonstrated a limited grasp” of the risks posed to the financial system from the private markets boom.

The report said the Treasury had an attitude of “passivity”, which was all the more surprising given the potential risks to the UK’s financial stability from shadow banking and private markets.

The rapid growth of private credit, which is corporate loans made by non-bank lenders, has raised concerns about its stability and the potential impact of a crash on the wider financial system.

Concerns about weakening lending standards were fuelled last year by the collapse of two US companies – First Brands, a car parts supplier, and Tricolor, a subprime auto lender.

Jamie Dimon, the head of JPMorgan, warned that more “cockroaches” would emerge because of lax lending standards over the years.

Lord Hollick, a member of the committee and former boss of media giant UBM, said: “The Treasury itself needs to be curious about this and needs to be actively engaged in looking for this and finding this information and giving us their view because obviously a lot of savers’ money, pension funds and insurance companies are going to be tied up with this.

“Heaven forbid that anything should go wrong, but clearly it’s of sufficient size and importance for the Treasury also to take a careful look at it.”

While the Lords committee praised the work of the Bank of England, led by Andrew Bailey, it warned that the Treasury appeared to defer to the Bank, with limited engagement with the issue itself.

The report concluded that there was not enough information to conclude whether private markets were “systemic” – in other words, whether they could pose a risk to the wider financial system.

In particular, it praised the Bank for its plan to conduct the world’s first “stress test” of the shadow banking sector to see how it would cope with a global shock.

The exercise will run over two rounds, with results expected to be published in early 2027.

While tougher regulations for banks following the financial crisis have boosted their resilience, they have also pushed some of their traditional lending activities outside the more regulated banking sector.

Britain’s private market has grown by 56pc since 2015 to $185bn (£138bn) and is the second-largest after the United States, the report said.

A Treasury spokesman said: “We have worked together with the regulators to significantly increase our focus of non-banks sectors in recent years and have a robust, flexible framework to protect financial stability.

“We are considering the committee’s recommendations and will respond in due course.”

data.title: Amazon axes 16,000 jobs amid push to replace office workers with AI
data.description:
Amazon is to lay off 16,000 workers in one of its biggest rounds of job cuts as the tech giant looks to replace office workers with artificial intelligence (AI).

The online retailer told staff on Wednesday that it would be cutting around 4pc of its workforce in an effort to reduce bureaucracy and compete in a “world that’s changing faster than ever”.

The redundancies come just three months after Amazon said it was letting go of a further 14,000 employees. The company’s warehouse workers, who account for most of its 1.5 million staff, are not affected.

News of the impending job cuts emerged early on Wednesday morning after an Amazon executive accidentally leaked an email about the lay-offs to employees.

Some staff were sent an email about “Project Dawn” detailing the planned lay-offs hours before Amazon had told its wider workforce.

It comes as Amazon increasingly turns to automation and AI in a push by Andy Jassy, the chief executive, to boost productivity.

White-collar workforce to fall

While the company’s latest email to employees did not mention AI, the lay-offs come after Mr Jassy said last year he expected Amazon’s total white-collar workforce would fall as it embraces the technology.

In June last year, Mr Jassy said: “We will need fewer people doing some of the jobs that are being done today, and more people doing other types of jobs.

“In the next few years, we expect that this will reduce our total corporate workforce as we get efficiency gains from using AI extensively across the company.”

Amazon has been driving staff to experiment with AI tools across their work at the company as rival Silicon Valley giants increasingly tie the use of digital chatbots and coding tools to performance reviews.

Companies have increasingly blamed AI for mass job cuts over the past year as they seek to offload more work to digital bots.

Data from hiring consultancy Challenger found that AI had been cited in lay-off announcements that resulted in more than 48,000 job cuts in 2025.

In her note to staff, Beth Galetti, Amazon’s vice-president of people, said: “As I shared in October, we’ve been working to strengthen our organisation by reducing layers, increasing ownership and removing bureaucracy.

“The reductions we are making today will impact approximately 16,000 roles across Amazon, and we’re again working hard to support everyone whose role is impacted.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Thames Water lenders prepared to take £7bn hit to avoid nationalisation
data.description:
A group of Thames Water creditors is prepared to effectively write off £7.5bn of debt in a bid to avoid nationalisation.

The consortium has proposed a turnaround plan that would cancel around a third of Thames Water’s near-£20bn debt pile and commit the group to investing in the company.

Outstanding fines would be paid – but the investor group, dubbed London & Valley Water, would also seek leniency on future penalties in exchange for investment.

Thames Water revealed over the summer that annual losses had hit £1.6bn as a soaring debt burden and continued sewage failures fuelled its financial crisis.

Bosses said it would take at least a decade to turn around Britain’s largest water company.

The company’s creditors are looking to secure backing for their plans to avoid the utility being put into a temporary special administration regime, which would effectively wipe out their investments.

Lenders said they would not pay a dividend while conducting their turnaround plans or until the company is listed.

They also committed not to sell the business before the end of March 2030.

The consortium said the “more ambitious” plan would increase investment in Thames Water by around £1bn compared to proposals they submitted to Ofwat in May.

The latest proposal was the result of “three months of detailed discussion” with the water regulator and would “support long-term financial resilience and is intended to help the company pay for outstanding fines”, it added.

Mike McTighe, a City veteran whose current roles include chairman of Telegraph Media Group, would become chairman of Thames Water under the creditors’ proposals.

He said: “There is a huge amount of work to be done to turn around Thames Water and deliver the improved service and environmental outcomes that customers and local communities deserve.

“From day one, we will inject billions in new investment, strengthen Thames Water’s balance sheet, transform the company for thousands of hard-working frontline staff and begin the delivery of an operational turnaround that puts 16 million customers and the environment first.”

Lenders of Thames Water’s “Class A” debt would receive at least 10pc of new equity in the utility company in exchange for writing off about 25pc of their stake, worth around £4bn.

Thames Water said it remains in discussions over the bid but any agreement would be subject to High Court approval.

Chris Weston, the chief executive of Thames Water, said: “Today is an important milestone in our ongoing work with creditors and stakeholders to secure a market-led recapitalisation that establishes the financial and regulatory foundations required to support the investment and performance improvements our customers expect.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: The towns where the taps ran dry
data.description:
On a grey Tuesday in East Grinstead, a little girl runs along the pavement to the redbrick town library. She stands at the automatic glass doors waiting for them to open, but nothing happens. A moment later her mother arrives. The woman looks at the sign that reads “library closed today”, then tells the child she will not be able to borrow any books after all.

Like almost every business or public building in this West Sussex town, the library is shut as a result of South East Water’s failure to keep the taps running.

The company’s bosses might just about be forgiven if this were a one-off. But it is the second officially declared major incident in South East Water territory in as many months, with customers in Tunbridge Wells, Kent, still facing disruption after weeks of problems.

South East has repeatedly fallen short on its most basic duty. In 2023, 6,000 households in Kent and Sussex went without water when supplies at South East ran out. In 2022, thousands of people spent Christmas with no water due to leakages.

The company, which serves 2.3 million customers, is now well on its way to claiming the crown of Britain’s worst utility from basket-case Thames Water, leaving its licence to operate under threat.

Businesses on the brink

Dave Hinton, the chief executive of South East Water, who took home £515,000 last year including a £115,000 bonus, was hauled in front of a parliamentary committee earlier this month. He had been summoned to answer questions on the crisis that hit Tunbridge Wells in December and ruined Christmas for 24,000 homes and businesses.

The spa town made an unlikely backdrop for a water crisis. From the 17th century, Tunbridge Wells’s mineral waters drew bathers and wealth.

But in the 21st century, water dealt an economic blow. Pub and bar owners in Tunbridge Wells told The Telegraph they had lost ruinous amounts of money.

“In four days we lost between £15,000 to £20,000. We are really struggling,” Bianca Omer, the manager of the cocktail bar Rocca said.

The bar survived. But Tudor Price, the chief executive of Kent’s chamber of commerce, believes that pubs, restaurants and bars in Tunbridge Wells “potentially lost about £20m of revenue” as a result of the outage.

Some are now on the brink. With many pubs already threatened by the Chancellor’s business rates raid, lengthy closure may deliver the final blow for some in Tunbridge Wells.

More alarmingly, a kidney treatment centre, 19 care homes, and two hospitals were left without water too.

‘Flying blind’

Debate has raged over whether the crisis in Tunbridge Wells was foreseeable. Hinton, perhaps unsurprisingly, reckons not.

At his parliamentary hearing he told Alistair Carmichael, the no-nonsense MP for Orkney and chairman of the environment select committee, that the whole thing was a bolt from the blue. In fact, because the crisis came as such a shock, Hinton insisted, he’d give himself “eight out of 10 in terms of response”.

The A-grade mark, as Carmichael put it, raised eyebrows among committee members. But when Marcus Rink, the boss of the Drinking Water Inspectorate, delivered his verdict, the benefit of the doubt evaporated.

“It shouldn’t have been a surprise,” Rink said. The water treatment works at nearby Pembury hadn’t just gone down for a few hours – it had been failing for some time, he explained.

“The start of the incident, in the opinion of my inspectors, was Nov 9.” With the Pembury plant the only water treatment works serving Tunbridge Wells, its continued good health is essential to the area.

Rink said South East was “flying blind”. The plant lacked electronic monitoring, relying on manual observations that missed failing filters.

Despite prior regulatory enforcement notices and missing micro-filtration units, the company had ignored weeks of visible deterioration, eventually leading to a totally avoidable system collapse.

The picture for South East became worse as Rink laid out more details. The company had been notified that there were issues with the treatment works a year previously. Testing had been intermittent, and the specialists monitoring the facility had all knocked off on Friday afternoon before returning on Monday morning. Nov 9 was a Sunday.

If MPs have been determined to hold Hinton to account, the same cannot be said of South East Water’s non-executive directors. Other managers have been sent out to face the music, among them a flustered Neil Hudson, South East’s head of water quality.

“What would happen to people if they do drink it?” a local BBC news reporter demanded to know after residents were advised to boil their water before using it.

“Any sort of normal, healthy individual will probably see no impact whatsoever. If anyone drinks the water and starts to feel unwell, our advice would be to speak to your GP,” Hudson said.

Asked by MPs why he had dodged the media, Hinton claimed journalists would not have asked relevant questions. They would have focused on his salary and South East Water’s finances, he complained.

When he finally came out of hiding, Hinton told ITV that he was “an operational CEO” and as such had spent the crisis participating in “traditional CEO activities”.

Henry Tuffnell, the young Labour MP for Pembrokeshire who sits on the environment committee, pointed out that for an “operational CEO” Hinton seemed to be remarkably short of operational detail.

Tuffnell tells The Telegraph: “You can’t say you were all over the detail, and therefore didn’t have time to do any media, if you weren’t over the detail.”

Even as Hinton faced MPs, back in Tunbridge Wells a second major incident was already in the offing. Still recovering from the pre-Christmas outage, locals were cut off again on Saturday.

Round two, according to South East, was due to “recent cold weather and a subsequent breakout of leaks and bursts”. This was no minor blip. Within days, 30,000 homes and businesses across Kent and Sussex had no water at all.

‘We have had enough’

In East Grinstead, four schoolboys kick a yellow football against the wall. They are the only people in the town who seem to think the outage is a good thing. “It’s a day off,” one of them offers cheerfully.

In Waitrose, almost all of the water has gone from the shelves except for a few bottles of San Pellegrino and some strawberry-flavoured Volvic. There was a water station in the car park the previous day, but it closed after 90 minutes.

East Grinstead council said: “We were not contacted directly by South East Water at any stage during the recent water supply disruption in the town. To the best of the council’s understanding, local knowledge was not utilised when setting up the initial water distribution stations.

“The town council regrets that it was not involved from the outset, as early engagement and local knowledge could have helped ensure a smoother, safer, and more coordinated response for the town’s residents.”

Local knowledge is clearly not South East Water’s strong suit. In December the company began its comedy of errors in Tunbridge Wells with the opening of a water station in Tonbridge, seven miles away.

For those affected, it’s hard to see the funny side. Gary, who lives homeless in East Grinstead, and had just two bottles of water left, told The Telegraph he was “scared”. Like many in the town, he wondered: “What is this all about?”

Jonathan Hawker, a Tunbridge Wells resident who set up the campaign group Dry Wells Action, says: “We can’t wash, flush toilets, do the dishes, wash clothes, top up boilers – all the normal stuff you take for granted – because of this abject failure by South East Water.

“We have had enough. We will now fight for our right to water. Tunbridge Wells is no longer disgusted. We are absolutely furious.”

Heavy hitters

South East’s board is now facing serious questions. Chris Train, the chairman, has been no more forthcoming about appearing publicly than his chief executive.

Described on the company’s website as bringing “a lot of experience”, he is also chairman of Sellafield, which in 2024 was fined for serious cyber security failings. The Commons public accounts committee criticised the nuclear power plant last year for “failure, cost-overruns and continuing safety concerns”.

After Hinton’s select committee mauling, Train offered a review by one of the non-executive directors.

Committee chairman Alistair Carmichael responded: “Members of the press and public would be forgiven for seeing Mr Train’s announcement of a further review of the Pembury failure as an attempt to buy themselves time, to hunker down until this storm blows over.

“They also assert that their review will be ‘independent’. It stretches the meaning of ‘independent’ when their review is to be conducted by a member of its board, supported by its staff. What was the investigation done by the Drinking Water Inspectorate if it was not an independent review?”

Hinton’s furious phone call

Desperate Tunbridge Wells residents have called for the Army to be brought in. Though that may sound a little over the top, there is logic to the request.

Hawker says: “The Army are logistics specialists who are used to dealing with challenging situations. We need experts who can manage logistics in emergency situations.”

South East Water officials can’t even manage a basic task like carting bottles of water from one place to another, he argues.

If Hinton has been the baddie, Mike Martin, the Liberal Democrat MP for Tunbridge Wells, has emerged as something of a local hero. He has had his sleeves rolled up throughout the process and repeatedly taken Hinton to task. This resulted in Hinton phoning Martin one evening during the eye of the storm to give him both barrels.

In his evidence to the environment committee, Martin said Hinton had accused him of politicising the crisis, asking: “How can you sleep at night?” The chief executive admitted to Carmichael that the telephone tantrum had not been his “finest moment”.

South East is more used to getting away with it.

As well as the four major shortages customers have endured on Hinton’s watch, South East Water has ranked in the bottom three companies for disruptions every year since Hinton took over. In 2023, it had the dubious accolade of recording the most interruptions and outages in England and Wales.

Its performance is getting worse, not better. In 2025, just 13 of it 38 annual targets were met – a little more than a third. Among the areas where it fell short were leakages, supply interruptions, water taste and odour, customer satisfaction and value for money. The previous two years, South East scored 15 – equivalent to less than 40pc. In 2022, it ranked positively on 24 of the 38 goals.

The company has suggested that its systems would be much better if only it had been able to raise customers’ bills, a familiar refrain in the water industry.

Yet it already has. Bills went up by 20pc just last year, meaning the average customer now pays £294 a year. It is also one of only five companies pushing to lift bills above the figure set by the regulator over the next five years.

Ofwat also gave short shrift to South East’s most recent investment plan. The company’s performance commitments were “of low to moderate ambition and do not go far enough in some cases”, the regulator said last April.

When it comes to “water supply interruptions, per capita consumption, repairs to burst mains and biodiversity”, the targets South East Water proposed “remained among the lowest across the sector”, Ofwat added.

South East responded by accusing the watchdog of being “unfair, disproportionate and punitive”. The company boasts of having “60 teams repairing leaks reactively and 86 leakage technicians and analysts searching for and finding leaks proactively”. More than 25,000 leaks were repaired in 2024, it says.

For any customers who feel they’re not getting value for money, the company points out: “The average drinking water bill now costs 81p per day, less than the cost of a second-class stamp.”

A spokesman said: “Our priority is improving South East Water in the future. We are strategically focused on our business plan and committed to delivering the infrastructure investment needed. This will improve our resilience as we face the challenges of climate change and population growth.”

It adds that Pembury’s water treatment works at the centre of the Tunbridge Wells crisis is due a full upgrade: “In advance of this full refurbishment, a number of improvements and changes are being made. Some of these are complete, and others are still ongoing, all of which are in line with the regulators’ timescales.”

Finances are underwater

South East’s financial performance is no less wretched than its operational performance. The company has failed to post a profit for the last three years, and though pre-tax losses roughly halved to £19.7m last year, they total £130m since 2022.

Weighed down with £1.3bn of borrowings – a figure that has steadily increased every year under Hinton – the cost of servicing those debts regularly eclipses the company’s profits. In the last five years, more than £400m has gone out of the door on debt costs and finance charges alone.

A further £168m has been paid out dividends, £136m of it in the form of a regulator-approved repayment of an internal loan to its parent company in 2020. South East Water is ultimately owned by a consortium comprising Australian and Canadian funds and NatWest’s pension fund.

In the two-year period from 2020 to 2022, the amount that South East paid out in dividends and debt interest surpassed what it spent upgrading infrastructure.

In 2024, the company revealed that it needed further cash to stay afloat. Ten months later, it received a £200m equity injection from shareholders.

Yet none of this has stopped Hinton receiving hundreds of thousands of pounds in pay and bonuses every year. Total earnings since assuming the top job in 2020 stand at an estimated £2.3m. Last year, he was paid a basic salary of £307,000, a £115,000 bonus and received a further £35,000 in pension contributions and other benefits.

A spokesman for South East said: “South East Water remains committed to a remuneration framework that supports a performance culture, and recognises success but does not reward poor performance.

“The bonus scheme is evaluated by the company’s independent non-executive director-led remuneration committee. The executive directors are not part of this committee and have no say in their remuneration.

“We can confirm that no bonus payment was made for operational performance for the year 2024-25. Details of all payments made can be found in our annual report on our website.”

‘Out of control’

Yet pinning the fiasco on a lack of investment when executives’ backs are against the wall doesn’t wash with Tunbridge Wells MP Mike Martin.

“Quite clearly, corporate governance has broken down,” he said. “We are now in a situation where a major public utility is out of control – failing its customers, risking public health and treating Parliament with contempt.”

If the board won’t step up, Martin thinks the utility’s bosses have to get involved. NatWest’s pension scheme owns 25pc of South East Water.

“NatWest Group Pension Fund will use its influence as a minority shareholder to direct South East Water’s board to ensure these issues are fully resolved,” it said in response to a letter from Martin this week.

The regulator has been spurred to action too. On Wednesday evening, the Government finally showed some mettle by calling on Ofwat to review South East Water’s licence to operate.

In an uncharacteristic show of speediness, on Thursday Ofwat announced an investigation into whether the supplier had met its obligations and offered support to those affected. Ofwat sceptics point out the regulator has been investigating South East since 2023.

There are local suspicions that Labour have, to a degree, let the crisis escalate because Tunbridge Wells and East Grinstead will never turn red. Richard Tice, the deputy leader of Reform UK, told The Telegraph that’s overthinking it. He said: “The problem is utter management incompetence at South East Water. My hunch is cock-up, not conspiracy.”

Yet the crisis is ongoing, and the inquisition is just getting started. By the water station at East Grinstead sports club, where the cars are backed up as far as you can see and people are pushing shopping trolleys full of bottles back home, a little boy on a bike asks if you can drink the water from the pond.

data.title: Inflation calculator: how the latest rate will affect your savings
data.description:
This calculator shows the current rate of Consumer Prices Index (CPI) inflation will affect the return on your savings.

Inflation calculator

How to use this calculator

Type in the amount you have in a savings account or cash Isa, and the interest rate you’re currently receiving.

Press the “Calculate” button to see the result.

How it works

This calculator works by figuring out how much interest your savings will earn over the course of a year – this assumes interest is paid annually, and the interest rate and amount of savings remains the same.

This is pitted against the most recent CPI measure of inflation, as reported by the Office for National Statistics.

Assuming inflation remains steady, it then shows the value your savings will lose in real terms due to inflationary price increases.

The calculator will also show you what the cumulative impact of these losses will be for each of the next five years.

What is this calculator useful for?

The calculator can help you gauge whether or not it’s worth switching to a savings account or cash Isa offering a higher rate of interest.

This is particularly important when inflation is high, but in general it’s a good idea to make sure you’re taking advantage of the best savings rates to reduce the effect of price increases. If you don’t, put simply, your money won’t buy as much as it could the previous year.

You can also use the calculator to see how much of a difference it would make to switch savings accounts for a better rate.

For example, someone with £20,000 in an account paying today’s average easy-access rate, according to Moneyfacts, of 2.45pc would make a “real terms” loss of £183.75 after a year because of inflation. They would earn £490 in interest, but their pot would effectively be worth just £19,816.25 after a year when adjusted for inflation.

Switching to the top easy-access savings rate which, according to today’s tables showing the best easy access savings accounts, is from Cahoot and pays 5pc, they’d make an inflationary increase of £348.84, based on actual interest of £1,000.

data.title: Bidding war rises over weight loss drug that could prevent ‘Ozempic face’
data.description:
Two of the world’s largest pharmaceutical companies are locked in a bidding war for the maker of a weight-loss drug that could help patients avoid succumbing to “Ozempic face”.

Danish giant Novo Nordisk has launched an attempt to gatecrash Pfizer’s $7.3bn (£5.6bn) takeover of US biotech Metsera by tabling an offer of up to $9bn.

Metsera, which had already agreed to the Pfizer deal, described the approach from Novo Nordisk as “superior”.

However, Pfizer hit back by branding the offer as “reckless”. The US pharmaceutical giant now has four working days to salvage a deal.

It comes as drug giants scramble to secure the newest and most innovative weight-loss treatments to meet soaring demand.

Metsera is developing treatments based on the hormone amylin, which researchers have suggested could stop people from losing muscle during rapid weight loss.

Currently, Eli Lilly’s Mounjaro and Novo Nordisk’s Wegovy help people to shed pounds, but also lead them to lose muscle as well as fat.

The same goes for Novo’s Ozempic, which is a diabetes drug used off-label for weight loss.

‘Ozempic face’

As well as being praised for the weight-loss credentials, the drugs have also been blamed for the rise of “Ozempic face”. This is where people receiving treatment start to appear gaunt as they lose muscle mass in their cheeks.

However, researchers have suggested that amylin could help people to maintain muscle even as they lose weight, as the hormone appears to play a role in maintaining body composition.

Dimitris Papamargaritis, an associate professor at the University of Leicester, earlier this year said: “Early lab studies suggest that adding amylin could help people lose more fat while sparing muscle, though this still needs confirmation in humans.”

Pharmaceutical companies have become increasingly aware of the issue as the drugs grow in popularity.

Sir Pascal Soriot, the AstraZeneca chief executive, last year said weight-loss medicines needed to be more targeted towards fat to help people avoid losing too much muscle in the long term.

He said: “Today you lose weight but you lose fat and you lose muscle. The problem with many of [the treatments] is when you stop, you regain weight, because obesity is a chronic disease.

“Most people, as soon as they stop taking the medicines, they regain fat, but not so much the muscle that they have lost unless, of course, they go to the gym.”

AstraZeneca is developing its own weight-loss treatments, with the aim of leapfrogging rivals with new, potentially cheaper, obesity drugs. In 2023, it bought a Chinese biotech which was working on a weight-loss pill.

Metsera is also developing pill-based weight-loss drugs, as well as longer-lasting treatments. It is testing one injection that people would only have to take once a month, compared to current weight-loss jabs that require once-a-week injections.

Novo Nordisk made the offer for the US biotech as it battles a slump in performance, having recently announced plans to cut 9,000 jobs.

data.title: Europe’s misguided energy policy is crushing industry
data.description:
Over the past decade, Europe’s weak economic performance has been blamed on familiar shocks: the global financial crisis, the eurozone debt crisis, Brexit and ageing populations.

Each have played a role – yet none explains why growth outcomes within Europe itself are now diverging so sharply.

Across the Continent, countries with access to cheap, reliable electricity are pulling ahead while those burdened with structurally high power costs are falling behind. Growth is weakening in Europe’s large core economies, notably the UK and Germany, while momentum is stronger in parts of the Nordics, Iberia and eastern Europe.

The result increasingly resembles a “doughnut” economy: strength at the edges, weakness at the centre.

This divergence reflects a widening competitiveness gap driven by differences in energy economics and above all, the cost of electricity.

At the turn of the millennium, electricity prices for households and industry in the US, the UK and Germany were broadly comparable.

Today, industrial electricity prices in the UK and Germany are four to five times higher than in the United States and materially higher than in other parts of Europe such as the Nordics and Iberia. Household prices are two to three times higher.

This is not a temporary distortion caused by the war in Ukraine or volatile global gas markets. Gas prices have largely normalised. Electricity prices have not.

The explanation lies in the structure of Europe’s energy systems and the policy choices that underpin them. In countries such as the UK and Germany, decarbonisation has relied heavily on capital-intensive, intermittent generation – particularly offshore wind – within electricity markets. While offshore wind has helped reduce emissions, it has also imposed large, system-wide costs.

These include long-term subsidy commitments, extensive grid reinforcement, curtailment and the backup generation and storage required to stabilise supply. Crucially, many of these costs are locked in for decades.

The economic consequences are hard to overstate.

High electricity costs act like a tax on the economy: on industry, on services and on households. Energy-intensive businesses face a stark choice – relocate or shut down. Industries that depend on abundant, cheap power, such as advanced manufacturing and AI data centres, will naturally gravitate towards lower-cost jurisdictions.

At the same time, higher household energy bills reduce disposable income, weighing on consumption and growth. The result is a slow but persistent erosion of competitiveness.

The critical point is that high electricity prices are not an inevitable feature of decarbonisation. This is not an argument against climate science or the need to reduce emissions, but the way the energy transition is executed matters enormously.

Historical evidence documented by thinkers such as Vaclav Smil, author of the magisterial book Energy and Civilization (which should be required reading for any selected MP entering the House of Commons), shows that cheap and abundant energy is a critical – perhaps the single most important – foundation for economic growth and development.

Too often, energy policy has been shaped by political and institutional incentives rather than by system-level engineering considerations, with insufficient attention paid to total system costs and their implications for national competitiveness.

Within Europe, there are clear examples of more pragmatic, engineering-led approaches.

In the Nordic countries, industrial electricity prices are close to US levels. Norway, Sweden and Finland benefit from vast hydroelectric resources, built decades ago and largely paid for.

They have also taken a pragmatic approach to nuclear power, retaining and in some cases expanding capacity rather than shutting it down. Combined with ample land for low-cost onshore wind, this mix delivers cheap and reliable electricity.

The gap in electricity prices between the Nordics and countries such as the UK and Germany is large, structural and unlikely to close any time soon.

Cheaper, more reliable power lowers operating costs across the economy, encourages capital investment and supports more energy-intensive activity, feeding through into higher productivity and faster growth over time.

Spain and Portugal offer a different but equally instructive model. Their energy strategies have been more balanced, retaining nuclear power and maintaining diversified gas supply routes. Crucially, they are heavily leveraged to solar power both at utility scale and on rooftops.

Solar is a deflationary technology: its costs continue to fall and its system-wide economics are fundamentally different from offshore wind. As a result, electricity prices in Spain and Portugal sit well below those in the UK, with clear implications for competitiveness and growth.

These differences are increasingly visible in economic performance. While forecasts vary, the pattern is consistent: the Nordics, Iberia and parts of eastern Europe are growing faster and more in line with economies that enjoy access to cheaper power.

Weak productivity and sluggish GDP growth in countries such as the UK and Germany are often attributed to abstract macroeconomic forces or one-off political events. But there is a simpler and more material explanation: years of rising electricity costs have quietly but steadily undermined competitiveness.

For investors and policymakers alike, the lesson is clear. Europe is not a single, homogenous economic bloc. It is a patchwork of countries with sharply diverging energy economics and increasingly diverging growth trajectories.

Where electricity is cheap and reliable, investment follows, productivity rises and growth proves more resilient. Where power is expensive and volatile, capital quietly goes elsewhere. Over time, these differences compound.

Europe’s emerging “doughnut” economy is not an accident, nor is it inevitable – but unless energy policy is re-anchored in system-wide economics as well as climate goals, the Continent risks locking in a form of structural stagnation.

Niall Gallagher is European equities investment manager at Jupiter Asset Management.

data.title: The German plot to woo Rolls-Royce
data.description:
Sitting in front of an enormous prototype jet engine, a German minister beams at the camera as he signs a cooperation deal with two top Rolls-Royce executives.

“With this, both sides commit to actively shape the transformation of aviation,” a social media post boasted gleefully afterwards.

Dietmar Woidke, the president of the German state of Brandenburg, had every reason to feel cheerful at November’s signing ceremony.

In the past decade alone, his state has attracted hundreds of millions of pounds in investment from Rolls and positioned itself as a “centre of excellence” for small-business jet engines.

Now it is jockeying for an even bigger prize – and a chance to swipe some of Britain’s industrial crown jewels.

In recent months, German officials have mounted a charm offensive to secure more work related to Rolls’ next-generation jet engine, the UltraFan.

Rolls-Royce Dahlewitz hub, outside Berlin, has already played a key role in developing what is arguably the new turbofan’s key innovation: its gearbox.

But the Germans are understood to be pushing hard for Dahlewitz to play an even larger role in the future, based on an offer of potentially generous subsidies.

The timing of these lobbying efforts is not coincidental either, coming as Berlin scrambles to defend itself from an avalanche of Chinese cars that threaten to crush Germany’s once-unstoppable motor industry.

Worryingly for Sir Keir Starmer, Rolls appears to be listening.

While the company is understood to favour keeping development in Britain, insiders insist it is duty-bound to consider all options – particularly as it is accountable to shareholders, not the Labour Government in London.

One well-placed industry source said: “The Germans are already involved in UltraFan. The only question now is what they do beyond that.”

Such a loss of investment by the company, widely regarded as a national champion, would deal a serious blow to Starmer’s industrial strategy and risk the loss of valuable know-how to foreign factories.

“It’s a very sensible thing for Rolls-Royce to consider – and potentially very bad for the UK,” warns Rian Whitton, an industry analyst at Bismarck Analysis.

The German advances have come at a critical moment, just as Rolls is considering a return to the fast-growing market for “narrow-body” planes, which have a single aisle and are used by carriers such as Ryanair for short-haul flights.

Rolls-Royce stopped producing narrowbody engines more than a decade ago. Embarrassingly, this meant the company completely missed a boom in demand for best-selling aircraft such as the Boeing 737 and Airbus A320.

But the company’s UltraFan demonstrator offers a route back in, thanks to the engine’s scalability.

Under the leadership of Tufan Erginbilgic, the company is preparing to spend £3bn on developing a narrowbody UltraFan demonstrator.

Rolls is understood to be seeking hundreds of millions of pounds in subsidies for the programme and has raised the issue at the highest levels of Government.

Sir Keir Starmer and Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, have visited the company’s Derby headquarters multiple times and used its factory as a backdrop for major announcements.

UltraFan could be a game-changer for several reasons, including improving overall efficiency by around 10pc compared to today’s engines. Engineers believe the design can be scaled up or down, meaning it could be used to power both narrow and so-called “wide-body” aircraft, which have two aisles between the seats.

Insiders acknowledge that the technology improvements also have clear potential applications in defence, such as increasing the capacity or flight time of air tankers, or enabling more compact fighter jet engines.

At a time of global instability and questions about the reliability of the US, such sovereign capabilities are seen as increasingly important.

Still, Germany has already played a crucial role in the development of the first UltraFan demonstrator.

Dahlewitz engineers co-created the power gearbox in a joint venture with domestic company Aerospace Transmission Technologies.

At the same time, Dahlewitz is where Rolls develops and manufactures the Pearl range of business jet engines and is developing new, even smaller engines for “loyal wingman” drones.

However, state and federal government officials are urging Rolls to consider assigning the German site a role in other parts of UltraFan as well.

That would be controversial in Britain, with sites such as Derby currently responsible for designing and developing sections, including the fans, core and combustion chamber.

Within these specialisms lie some of the most valuable and closely guarded secrets in the British aerospace industry.

Jobs in this field are also high-value and distributed geographically across the UK, with any loss of production to foreign factories likely to be politically controversial.

“These jobs are a relatively small component of overall employment, but they are disproportionately high productivity,” Bismarck’s Whitton adds.

“They are important to exports and research and development spending, so they should arguably be prioritised over measures that just increase employment more generally.”

‘Aviation is becoming an important offset’

But Germany is equally desperate to pull them in, as its traditional strongholds are increasingly threatened by a flood of cheap goods from China, says Sander Tordoir, chief economist at the Centre for European Reform.

“German chemicals and carmaking, which is still Germany’s most important industry, are really in deep trouble,” he says.

“So aviation is becoming an important offset – it’s a sector that’s actually growing.”

According to the German Aerospace Industries Association, the number of aerospace jobs rose to 120,000 in 2024, up from around 105,000 a decade earlier.

The country’s aviation industry has long been anchored by Airbus, which conducts final assembly of some aircraft there and also manufactures fuselage sections, cabins, and wing parts domestically.

Germany is also home to Rolls-Royce’s power systems business, which manufactures diesel and gas generators, as well as engines for ships and trains, among other things.

But the country currently lacks a champion capable of making complete commercial jet engines – a weakness that has left it exposed in a row with France over who will get to make what for a next-generation warplane.

This might also partially explain why German officials are so keen to build up this particular muscle, along with the threat from China and the high value of aviation jobs, says Tordoir.

“Airbus is a German-British-French-Spanish collaboration and has done very well. But of course, that’s not how politicians will think of it in the short run,” he adds.

Certainly, some alarm bells should be ringing in Whitehall, according to Bismarck’s Whitton.

But he questions whether ministers and their officials are prepared to act, despite Rolls having recently secured substantial work funded by UK taxpayers.

Rolls was recently chosen by the Government to design the country’s first mini nuclear power plants and has separately won orders for specialist military reactors that will be needed for the Royal Navy’s Dreadnought and Aukus-class submarines.

But in some ways, the fact that Rolls is the sole supplier of some of these technologies gives the Government less leverage in the dispute, not more.

There is also little cash available for handouts, given the constrained state of public finances, whereas in Germany, the government is committing significant spending to defence and infrastructure.

“I think in France, this just wouldn’t happen,” says Whitton. “In part because [national aviation champion] Dassault is family-run and has significant state ownership.

“But unfortunately, Whitehall does not have a tremendously strong hand to play here.

“We talk about Rolls-Royce as a British crown jewel, but that’s got to be heavily caveated. Today it is a fairly globalised, export-led, publicly traded company that does not have a primary loyalty to the British state.”

Still, he argues ministers should fight hard to keep these jobs here.

“They need to have a tough conversation with them,” he says.

The Government has so far attempted to dismiss such concerns. A spokesman said the UK has one of the most competitive aerospace sectors in the world and that it values “the role Rolls-Royce continues to play, supporting thousands of high-quality, high-wage jobs”.

Yet with Germany on Britain’s tail, ministers may soon wish they had paid more attention.

A Rolls-Royce spokesman said the company had a “long-standing relationship with the State of Brandenburg” and looked forward to “building on our successful collaboration in the future”.

data.title: Industry sacrificed over net zero ideology, says Siemens Energy boss
data.description:
Europe is sacrificing industry to net zero “ideology”, the chairman of Siemens Energy has warned.

Joe Kaeser said strict zero-emission targets had crippled manufacturing and heavy industry in Germany and beyond. He called on the German government to work on a more “economically feasible plan” for the country to decarbonise.

“The critics have a point when they say you risk your economic power [and] society’s wellbeing by being ideological about net zero,” he said.

His comments will raise questions about Britain’s approach to net zero, which official forecasts show will add hundreds of billions of pounds to government spending over the next few decades.

As well as questions about cost, the UK and Europe face increasing pressure from the US to dial back plans which Trump administration officials claim are making countries “subservient” to Beijing.

Sir Keir Starmer will fly to China this week in the first visit by a British prime minister since 2018, with energy likely to be among the topics discussed.

Mr Kaeser, who previously ran German industrial giant Siemens before becoming chairman of its energy division, warned that European companies were becoming uncompetitive because of green energy policies.

He criticised the government in Berlin for forcing zero emission targets on carmakers and demanding that businesses prove the products they sell have no connection to deforestation.

The German businessman also criticised officials for sacrificing reliable, cheap energy by ditching fossil fuels.

Mr Kaeser said: “They became ideologists on renewables. That’s a bad idea. If you are an industrial country and cannot provide a sustainable, affordable and reliable source of energy, you have a problem.”

Germany’s share of global emissions is currently less than 2pc – much lower than other industrialised countries including Brazil and India – and Mr Kaeser said even halving this figure would make little difference to the global picture.

By comparison, China now accounts for a third of global carbon emissions while the UK’s share is less than 1pc.

“Why would you risk your industrial competitiveness by maybe being a rounding error in the global discussion?” Mr Kaeser said.

Germany has a goal of hitting zero carbon emissions by 2045, a more aggressive target than Britain’s legally enshrined 2050 deadline.

Mr Kaeser, who is also the chairman of Daimler trucks, attacked the EU’s approach of reaching net zero by regulation. He said Brussels spent too much time drawing up rules for manufacturers without considering how they could kill demand.

Under current targets, 43pc of Europe’s heavy goods vehicle fleet must be electric by 2030, 65pc by 2035 and 90pc by 2040. Manufacturers who fail to comply face huge fines.

“The EU regulates everything,” Mr Kaeser said. “It regulates the supply side. So the EU tells us ... we don’t want the diesel truck any more. They don’t go to the customer and ask the demand side, what are you up to? What does your business case look like?”

The Siemens Energy chairman was speaking at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland, where world leaders and business elites gathered this week. While much of the event was dominated by Donald Trump’s threats over Greenland, US officials also used the summit to criticise Europe’s net zero policies.

Mr Trump used his speech to attendees to rail against “the green new scam”.

Howard Lutnick, the US commerce secretary, warned that Europe was becoming “subservient” to China because it was reliant on Beijing for products such as electric car batteries.

Most of Britain’s solar panels currently come from China and Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary, is also driving a rapid expansion of UK wind power. Chinese companies dominate in the production of wind turbines.

Siemens Energy, which operates a factory in Hull, is still dealing with the fallout from a crisis in its wind division caused by quality issues and soaring production costs. The disaster forced the German government to bail the company out in 2023. Mr Kaeser said the company remained committed to turning the unit around and making it profitable again.

By contrast, orders for its gas turbines are booming as demand for AI data centres grows.

data.title: Thames Water’s rescue plan risks setting a dangerous precedent
data.description:
The latest version of a creditor-led rescue plan for Thames Water ticks multiple boxes. One can see why the company, ministers and the industry regulator might be minded to give it the green light in the coming weeks.

The pressure to hammer out a deal is intense. The threat of a multibillion-pound bill landing on Rachel Reeves’s desk before her Budget in November is concentrating Whitehall minds.

Thames is burning cash rapidly. A separate announcement from the company revealed that in August it called on the remaining £628m of a £1.5bn emergency loan.

It is further worrying evidence of the extent to which an organisation providing critical services to 13 million people continues to live hand-to-mouth after years of reckless ownership.

With the clock ticking, the “London and Valley” consortium of lenders has come up with a decent upgrade on the somewhat vague proposal published a month ago.

Then, the prospective new owners declared their undying commitment to “a new transparent and collaborative relationship with regulators”, before outlining a plan lacking in details.

The highlights of their latest attempt include a more generous portion of equity and a bigger debt write-off than previously envisaged. A commitment to not pay dividends for five years, along with the ambition of eventually returning Thames to the stock market are also welcome measures.

Yet all the hard work that has gone into answering many of the concerns of regulators, Government and long-suffering customers risks being overshadowed by what amounts to a fresh attempt to wriggle out of fines.

Having sensibly given up on trying to dodge outstanding penalties for pumping sewage into waterways, Thames’s wannabe owners are now trying to inoculate themselves against future punishment.

“The current regulatory framework for the water industry does not allow it to successfully turnaround its business,” London and Valley says.

The consortium won’t take “no” for an answer, it seems.

Steve Reed, the former environment secretary, told parliament in June that Thames must meet its statutory obligations, after being asked about possible “regulatory easements”.

But the very idea that an industry that treats Britain’s rivers as its dumping ground should face softer environmental targets – or “stretching but achievable incentives” in the consortium’s words – won’t wash.

It’s hard to square this with the consortium’s warm words.

Mike McTighe, the proposed future chairman of Thames – he is also chairman of the company behind The Telegraph – talks about “rebuilding public trust”.

The creditors themselves admit that Thames has presided over “a cycle of underinvestment and underperformance that has driven trust in Thames Water to an all-time low.”

Few would argue with that assessment. Yet any agreement that makes it easier for the company to pollute with greater impunity is an improbable basis for the restoration of Thames’s relationship with the public.

Consider that in an industry that has vandalised the environment, this is a chronic offender.

Thames’s record is getting worse, not better. Some 50pc more raw sewage was discharged into Britain’s waterways from Thames’s ageing system last year, compared with the previous 12 months.

In May, Thames Water was fined £123m by Ofwat – the biggest ever fine imposed by the regulator.

From a purely financial perspective, the creditors’ plea for clemency is understandable.

They expect future fines of more than £1bn if current regulations are applied and argue that something has to give if Thames is to receive the investment it needs.

This financial hole was of Thames’s own making, however. Its creditors bankrolled it as it hollowed itself out. To turn around now and demand that the rules are changed to make pumping effluent into rivers a less costly business is an affront to justice and risks setting a horrible precedent.

Perhaps even now, after months and months of negotiations, the hedge funds that swooped in to buy up debt on the cheap when Thames was on the brink, still don’t understand the extent of public and political anger.

Customer ire stretches to the whole gamut of misdeeds – rising bills, poor service, undeserved dividends and excessive executive pay.

But it is the epidemic of pollution that has attracted the most fury. That the creditors have chosen to attack on that front seems “courageous”, as they say in the Civil Service.

Indeed, I am willing to bet that the majority of people want to see stiffer fines, not softer ones, particularly if the current set-up has failed to act as a deterrent. Weaker rules would surely lead to more pollution, not less.

Meanwhile, the knock-on effect of granting Thames an easier ride would be that every other water company in Britain would be entitled to expect the same exemptions on the basis of fairness.

A reminder from the creditors that their offer is the only credible one left after private equity firm KKR abandoned its bid looks designed to focus minds. Ditto talks of how the plan “will avoid special administration”, and ensure that “Thames Water’s turnaround will not fall to the UK taxpayer”.

Decency, however, demands that Ofwat and the Government don’t allow themselves to be pushed around by a bunch of opportunistic hedge funds.

data.title: The home renovation dream is dead – it’s just too expensive
data.description:
Are you a landlord looking to sell up because of Labour’s baffling policies? Email us your about your experience at money@telegraph.co.uk*.

I knew when I bought my Art Deco dream home in May 2024 that I was going to be in for a rollercoaster ride.

It hadn’t been touched for decades. I knew the renovation costs would soar. I knew the timeframes would shift. And I knew I faced significant challenges (on top of the rental properties I already own) that would make me wonder why I ever decided to take out a bigger mortgage and sign on the dotted line.

But I have a love for period property. It wasn’t listed, thankfully, and was in a dated but liveable condition. After many years of renovating for others, I realised it was time to apply my knowledge and skills.

Refurbishing a property is a particularly British quirk – especially for my generation (X as I was born in 1975). For us, buying a property to “do up” and moving up the ladder was a property owner’s rite of passage.

But what I’ve come to realise – some 25 years after buying my first property – is that home renovation is now an elitist hobby where only those with money and know-how can succeed.

I first realised the challenges when I tried to buy a decent bathroom suite. I wasn’t aiming for anything extravagant, but I wanted something better than a rental standard. A quote came in at over £5,000 for just the suite and the taps. And that was with a trade discount. It didn’t help that VAT amounted to around £1,000 of that sum.

Several variations of the quote followed: swapping out taps, vanity units, baths, and before I knew it, the bathroom design had deviated so much from my original plan that I was no longer certain I wanted it.

But then the next reality set in – this was just the cost of the basics. I still had to add the cost of the tiles, the labour for fitting and tiling, the new radiator, the plastering, the electrics, the paint and the flooring.

All-in, the bathroom budget was now well more than £12,000, and if I was being honest, probably closer to £15,000.

Then there are hidden costs, such as waste removal. Since my local recycling centre does not accept tiles or bath suites, the only option was to hire a skip at an additional cost of £450.

I know that problems always arise when doing building work, so I was prepared with a contingency budget. But what I wasn’t expecting was to learn that my 45-year-old, fully functional (but admittedly not very efficient) boiler and water cylinder were incompatible with the new, modern taps.

Four plumbers offered a variety of solutions, ranging from keeping the old system and adding a pump, to changing the entire system, replacing with either a combi, an accumulator, a pumped accumulator or a stored combination boiler.

The assortment of options on offer has bewildered me, along with the prices, which are all over £4,000.

Given I hadn’t been planning to touch the heating and hot water system (I was saving for doing that next year), the learning curve has been steep.

Even if there had been any surplus funds, I would have used them to upgrade the two 30-year-old aluminium windows in the bathroom. However, having understood that I can’t have it all, I made peace with the fact that they were double-glazed and added them to a separate wish list for the future.

Typically, when it seems a project cannot go further off track, new challenges may still arise and test your abilities. In my case, a roof leak into the bathroom ceiling that was fixed in March 2025 at great expense – scaffolding, chimney repointing and new tiles quickly added up to thousands – returned two weeks before my project began.

The original roofer denied responsibility and refused to become further involved.

So now I wait for another roofer, a new start date, and search for spare change to cover the next bill.

At this point, I completely understand why councils are selling off properties that need updating. The economics are insane.

For me, I’ve had to reignite my love of DIY. It is the only cost-effective way I can get things done.

Although I am skilled with tools, I am surprised by the rising cost of everyday essentials. I remember when masking tape was available in packs of six for a very low cost – now they are £5 each!

Paint prices are another cause for upset. Buying just two sample pots cost me £12.98 (with trade discount), so unfortunately, Farrow & Ball paint can’t be on my agenda.

I know I’ll get there in the end. And maybe, in time, I’ll say all the stress, hassle and expenditure were worth it. But what concerns me more is how the economics of home renovation has been turned on its head.

Finding a property that needed refurbishment used to be a way to buy at a discount. It used to be how you moved up the ladder. Nowadays, it has become a luxury few can afford.

*Please note that by submitting your content to us, you are consenting to The Telegraph processing your personal data where required by law. For further details, please see our Privacy Notice.

data.title: Spanish train crash caused by ‘gap in track’
data.description:
Experts investigating the cause of a train crash in Spain that killed 40 people have found a broken joint on the rails.

The tail end of a train heading from Malaga to Madrid derailed at 7.45pm on Sunday, smashing into an oncoming train travelling from the capital to Huelva.

Investigators at the scene identified some wear on the joint between sections of the rail, known as a fishplate, which they said showed the fault had been there for some time, a source briefed on the investigations told Reuters.

They ‍found that the ‌faulty joint created a gap between the rail sections that widened as trains continued to travel along the track, which was renovated in May as part of a €700m (£608m) project.

Technicians believe the faulty joint is key to identifying the precise cause of the high-speed crash, the source added.

The first carriages of the train that derailed, operated by Spanish firm Iryo, drove over the gap in ‌the rails. But the eighth, ‍and last, carriage derailed, bringing with it the seventh and sixth carriages, the source said.

Authorities are yet to establish an official cause of the crash, with Oscar Puente, the Spanish transport minister, calling the collision “truly strange”.

The Iryo train was less than four years old and passed an inspection on Jan 15.

Álvaro Fernández Heredia, the president of Renfe, the operator of the second train, ruled out the possibility of human error or excessive speed.

The speed limit on the track is 250km/h (149mph), while the trains were travelling at 210km/h and 205km/h when they collided.

In the months leading up to the crash, the company that manages Spain’s railway infrastructure warned of at least eight technical incidents on the high-speed line.

Most of the problems related to the signalling system, although there were also issues related to overhead power lines and other infrastructure.

The government was asked about the issues last summer and responded by saying it had addressed two “technical incidents affecting the signalling systems”.

In a statement, Iryo said it “deeply regretted what had happened” and that it had activated “all its emergency protocols”.

Authorities said there were 20 seconds between the Iryo train veering off the track and the subsequent collision, leaving the driver of the second train – who was killed in the crash – with almost no time to react before the impact.

Mr Heredia said the signalling and track-based safety mechanisms designed to detect an occupied track had no time to activate.

He also warned that determining the exact cause of the crash could take several days and that the circumstances were “rare and difficult to explain”.

The investigation is being led by the Commission for the Investigation of Railway Accidents, which operates within Spain’s transport ministry.

A separate judicial investigation has also been launched by a court in Montoro, Cordoba.

Juanma Moreno, Andalucia’s regional president, said: “It is likely [that more dead will be found] when you look at the mass of metal that is there. The firefighters have done a great job, but unfortunately, when they get the heavy machinery to lift the carriages, it is probable we will find more victims.

“Here at ground zero, when you look at this mass of twisted iron, you see the violence of the impact.”

Mr Moreno said that authorities were also searching the area nearby. “The impact was so incredibly violent that we have found bodies hundreds of meters away,” he added.

Video released by Spain’s Guardia Civil showed the sides of the worst-hit carriages shredded open, with train seats lying on the surrounding gravel.

One carriage lay on its side, bent around a large concrete pillar, with debris scattered around the area.

“There were moments when we had to remove the dead to get to the living,” Francisco Carmona, firefighter chief of Cordoba, told Onda Cero radio.

Javier Mesones, a 36 -year-old painter from Adamuz, said he heard about the crash on a WhatsApp group and set off with a friend to see if they could help.

The Guardia Civil did not let them access the crash zone in their car so the pair returned with a quad bike that became an unlikely rescue vehicle.

“There were people struggling to get clear of the wreckage and lying injured, so we pulled them onto the front rack of the quad and got them up the slope to safety – about 15 people,” Mr Mesones told The Telegraph.

“It was dark, sirens were blazing but what I saw was a catastrophe, a bloodbath. I’ve never seen anything like it in my life,” he added.

As well as the pain from wounds and injuries, passengers suffered from cold and shock on a night where the temperatures at the crash area dropped as low as 2C.

Agustín Pérez, another Adamuz resident, broke open a fence to allow vehicles access to a track that runs parallel to the railway to grant access to ambulances and rescue teams.

The 44-year-old owner of a wildlife tourism company said: “The thing that hit me hardest were the screams of pain from the carriages.”

Amid the chaos, Guardia Civil rescue workers found a six-year-old girl wandering alone with barely a few scratches on her head.

On Monday it emerged that the rest of the girl’s four family members who were travelling home to Punta Umbría in Huelva had died.

A sports centre in Adamuz was turned into a makeshift hospital, and the Spanish Red Cross established a help centre for emergency service workers and people seeking information.

“The scene was horrific. It was terrible,” Rafael Moreno, the mayor of Adamuz, told reporters. “People asking and begging for help. Those leaving the wreckage. Images that will always stay in my mind.”

Spanish King Felipe VI expressed his condolences, adding that the royal house was looking to visit Adamuz in the coming days.

“I understand the desperation of the families and the number of injured people who have suffered this accident, and we are all really worried,” he said.

On Monday, the Spanish flag was flown at half-mast in front of Madrid’s Parliament, while Pedro Sánchez, the country’s prime minister, visited the crash site.

Spain has spent decades investing in high-speed trains and currently has the largest rail network in Europe for trains over 155mph, with more than 1,900 miles of track, according to the European Union.

Sunday’s crash was the first with deaths on a high-speed train since Spain’s high-speed rail network opened its first line in 1992.
