data.title: The £11bn rail upgrade that will take 30 years to finish
data.description:
When Labour excitedly announced “the biggest transformation to travel in the North in a generation”, you’d have forgiven rail commuters for asking: “Which generation?” 

For all the government’s trumpeting last week of Northern Powerhouse Rail (NPR), and of £45bn to be allocated to railway station improvements, new routes and upgraded lines, it didn’t take much unpicking to notice that some of the work won’t even start for another 20 years. No new track is likely to be laid this decade. Most of today’s commuters will probably have retired by the time these schemes finally welcome trains. If indeed they ever do.

“If you look at what was actually committed to in the announcement,” sighs transport commentator Christian Wolmar, “there’s some great ideas, but the first £1bn set aside is in yet more business cases and planning for Northern Powerhouse Rail – a scheme that was first mooted by George Osborne in 2013.

“Another £1bn before a spade even goes in the ground. It’s just insane.”

Meanwhile, Westminster sources tell us that even though local mayors such as Greater Manchester’s Andy Burnham, West Yorkshire’s Tracy Brabin and South Yorkshire’s Oliver Coppard have publicly supported the plans after pushing for NPR for some time, it’s an incredibly fragile peace.

As one MP with a transport portfolio admits: “If it ends up being an expensive plan that doesn’t deliver a single mile of railway, then there’ll quickly be dissension in the ranks.” 

It was interesting that at Wednesday’s announcement, Rachel Reeves promised that lessons had been learnt from past rail infrastructure issues, such as HS2. The ongoing £11bn Transpennine Route Upgrade (TRU) scheme was cited as an example of a rail project which is proceeding on time and on budget.

And as the TRU’s managing director, James Richardson, stares up at a welder preparing the steelwork for a massive new £60m railway viaduct crossing the Calder and Hebble Navigation waterway in Ravensthorpe, West Yorkshire, he is keen ram home the message that this time things will be different. 

“It’s incredible work we’re doing,” says Richardson of the scheme to improve railway lines and infrastructure between York and Manchester.

“We need to deliver today for the capability of tomorrow.

“Tomorrow” is, though, the nebulous term here. As work on the TRU ticked over into 2026, it marked a staggering 15 years until it is due to be completed. If you take the railway upgrade plan of 2011 as the TRU’s first green signal light, we’re only halfway through what you’d forgive the people who use this line, calling an interminable 30-year process to improve just 70 miles of track; the full completion date is currently 2041.

Still, some TRU projects have now been completed – although even then, it took 10 years from the initial planning to major works beginning in 2021. And even now, with multiple sites in play, there’s still bureaucracy to navigate: late last year, it was announced that upgrading just seven miles of track between Stalybridge and Diggle would cost £225m – and that the Government had committed to funding the design work of this stage, but not yet its delivery.

Network Rail – which is ultimately responsible for this project – still has to apply for Transport and Works Act orders to get these projects lined up. The general consensus is that everyone prefers to work in this way because it’s easier to compartmentalise specific improvements and stage funding. That’s effectively the way NPR is likely to operate, too.

But you can see why industry experts are suggesting that if this were London – or anywhere else in the world, for that matter – we wouldn’t even be waiting for the funding to be allocated; the improvements probably would have been finished.

“You can take almost any European country and see the difference in approach,” says Wolmar, “They build a high-speed line, realise it’s a success and then develop a delivery agency to connect their cities.

“They don’t bother with detailed business cases and fancy bridges – they standardise the approach and the design, and build a whole network of high-speed lines for a similar amount that we’re talking about for NPR”.

For the TRU at least there is some mitigation as to why it’s taking so long. This isn’t a new line, it’s an upgrade which needs to do so much more than simply make do and mend Victorian infrastructure.

Right on cue, Richardson points out a plaque on the current bridge dating back to 1847.

“So we do recognise there is a huge desire to get the upgrades in place as quickly as possible, because of the benefits it will bring,” he admits. “But we have to balance that with the fact that we have passengers going through our network every day. We’re servicing around 15 million people in the North, and we’re actually constrained more by how much access we can get to the railway to deliver the works than the construction elements themselves.

“But the benefit of this staged approach is that there won’t be a decades-long wait for some kind of big-bang opening. We can deliver phased improvements and unlock elements such as timetable changes or station developments as we go.”

To that end, the TRU is keen to highlight that a quarter of the route is already electrified, and it’ll be up to 40 per cent by the summer of 2027.

“The headline news is always about reduced journey times, and we will deliver that,” says Richardson. “But what I think is more important here, what will be a step change for anyone travelling across the Pennines, is that this work will soon unlock around 300 extra services a week and make everything far more reliable too.

“People will be able to have confidence that they will make that flight or that meeting. That’s our vision for TRU, anyway, that we can make a really huge change for the North of England.”

And perhaps that’s also one of the many hard-won lessons from the HS2 debacle. The intention isn’t just to make a faster railway here, Richardson argues, it’s the result of listening to commuters and regional leaders who want services to be on time, and that when they go to a newly accessible railway station, it’ll be comfortable, not just a windswept, falling-down platform. When they step on to the train, too, there should be a seat in a modern train.

That way, thinks Richardson, with community buy-in extending to using local construction workers and companies, then major, long-term infrastructure projects such as this are less likely to be at the mercy of the prevailing political winds.

All of which actually sounds promising for NPR. But we’ve been here before. By continually funding expensive strategic plans rather than delivery, projects such as Northern Powerhouse Rail will always be at the mercy of politics. Reform, for example, has in the past been against some of the major NPR projects – one of the reasons that some think Labour announced this investment as part of its much-trialled 2026 reset.

Indeed, Jerome Mayhew, the shadow rail minister, accepts that something needs to change in the way these plans are endlessly talked about by governments of every persuasion, but is not impressed that a Labour Government which promised to get major infrastructure projects moving is instead “lurching from review to review, deadline to deadline”.

Henri Murison is the chief executive of Northern Powerhouse Partnership, a business-led think tank and advocacy group focusing on connectivity, devolution and education; George Osborne is its co-president. We spoke to him before the NPR announcement, and it was telling how delighted he was by the progress the TRU was making – and how exasperated he was at the general glacial pace of change.

“TRU may have been a long time in the planning – and decisions could have been made with more haste – but it’s now into delivery, and they’re doing so on time and on budget. It’s a rare example, actually, of a successful project,” he said.

“But with Northern Powerhouse Rail, the plan has been worked on for so many years. The Government needs to stop talking about it and get on with it.

“Labour promised in opposition that this would happen, and we’ve been patiently waiting, but at some point, you’ve got to say the North of England has been underinvested in for decades – and we need this to happen now.”

So it was fascinating that at the recent announcement, Murison spoke of NPR finally unlocking potential, while still highlighting the need for improved “global connectivity across the North”. He was effectively talking about a new high-speed Manchester Airport station – but that could be decades off, if not longer.

As one rail industry insider tells us: “The idea that they’re ever going to build that line between Manchester and Liverpool is pure fantasy, because it’s not a priority.”

And will Andy Burnham get the underground station for Manchester that, when we speak to him on a busy platform at a chilly Manchester Piccadilly just before Christmas (and his current attempt to get back into Westminster politics), he’s keen to secure?

Ostensibly, we meet because he’s launching a new Bee Network train – the plan being to move Greater Manchester services run by train companies such as Northern and TransPennine Express into an integrated transport network much like Transport for London. But, face to face, you could sense the frustration in his voice that, however clearly he’s expressed a vision for not just Greater Manchester but the North in general to his Labour Party colleagues, the amount of horse-trading going on in Whitehall was proving exhausting.

“Yes, there’ve been sticking points,” he admitted. “But we cannot trade down the economic and social ambitions of Greater Manchester; we are the fastest-growing city region in the country. We deserve the same highest standard of infrastructure to support that growth.”

Were those ambitions met with the Government’s announcement? For now, perhaps Burnham is happy enough. But it was telling how guarded the rest of his statement was. There wasn’t really a green light to an underground station in Manchester, but, as he put it, “an openness” to it being developed, which the city would have to “work at pace” to prove the case for.

It may even, as part of the NPR proposals, have to fund some of the improvements itself over and above the £45bn cap, perhaps through raising business rates (as Crossrail did).

Back at Ravensthorpe, however, the work on the viaduct and the new railway station here that has been approved, costed and paid for continues. “We haven’t seen this level of investment across the North,” says the TRU’s James Richardson.

The £45bn for Northern Powerhouse Rail sounds a lot, but ultimately it still remains something of a vague aspiration until work actually begins. Where money has finally been spent, on the TRU, it’s taken too long to get to delivery.

“Well, passengers on the TRU route have already seen and will continue to see noticeable improvements very quickly,” counters Richardson. “But what we need to do now is make sure we make the right decisions so that Northern Powerhouse Rail can happen in the future.”

data.title: Revealed: how much a nearby new estate really knocks down your house price
data.description:
Do you own a home near a new development? We want to hear about your experience, good or bad. Email money@telegraph.co.uk*

So-called “Nimbys” have become a scapegoat, accused of standing in the way of Labour’s vision to build 1.5 million homes by the end of this parliament.

But a Telegraph investigation reveals that they do so with good reason: new housing developments really do have a detrimental effect on the values of existing homes in the area.

House prices in the English countryside fell by £7,000 on average after major developments were built within half a mile, analysis shows, compared with a £3,000 rise for other homes in the area.

Rural homes within eyeshot of new developments decreased in value by 2.7pc over the past four years. By contrast, house prices in the same towns but further away grew by 1.3pc.

It comes as the Government introduces measures to speed up building in an attempt to meet its housing targets.

The homes that take a hit

The majority of Britons support, in principle, a large increase in the amount of new housing, according to a YouGov poll which found that 52pc were in favour compared with 37pc who were not.

However, when it comes to house building in their local area, that support turns to opposition.

In densely packed cities, residents generally understand that new housing projects bring necessary urban renewal. An ageing estate in poor condition may be torn down to make way for a modern development, or a disused industrial site turned into an affordable housing hub.

But it’s a different story in the countryside.

Opponents of building projects – often referred to as Nimbys, which stands for “not in my backyard” – believe that they spoil the British countryside, disturb the bucolic quiet, damage existing homeowners’ views or quality of life and put strain on local services and amenities. They argue that their property prices will fall if their rural homes become less desirable because of nearby modern developments and busier local populations.

The Telegraph’s analysis, using official transaction data from HM Land Registry, looked at new-build sales in villages and towns with a population of below 20,000.

It included only areas with a minimum of 50 new housing transactions in 2024 that were within a quarter-mile radius of each other to ensure that the housing project was significant enough to change the local landscape. The same threshold was applied to the number of established home sales in the neighbourhood.

There were 14 areas that met these criteria, covering the breadth of England from Essex to Yorkshire to Northumberland.

In 10 of those areas, house price growth was weaker near the new-build homes compared with the rest of the area. In nine of those 10 areas, homes near new estates lost value.

Skelmanthorpe, six miles outside the Peak District National Park in West Yorkshire, is the most extreme example of the trend.

In 2024, Land Registry transaction records indicated a cluster of 53 new-build sales within a quarter-mile radius. On a former patch of grassland on the town’s west side, the new suburb of Weavers Place had sprouted.

In the two years before this, existing detached and semi-detached homes within half a mile of the new development sold for an average of £361,000. In the two years after, this had plunged 18.5pc to £295,000.

Slightly further out in Skelmanthorpe – between half and three quarters of a mile from the site, and so with access to all the same services, bus connections and green space – prices rose by 5.9pc over the same period.

Nigel Bishop, of buying agency Recoco Property Search, said: “The majority of buyers wanting to purchase a home in the countryside seek quiet and natural surroundings.

“A new-built complex in eyeshot of their desired property would very much disturb the peace and aesthetics associated with country living, making the property less desirable to house hunters.”

He also explained that potential buyers could be concerned about additional developments being built in the future.

He said: “House hunters would then also be concerned about the area’s general infrastructure and how such developments impact the existing drainage system, Wi-Fi signal and whether the roads can accommodate increased traffic.

“The fear of the unknown and what is yet to come will cause buyers to walk away.”

Skelmanthorpe may be the most extreme example from the dataset, but it is by no means the only countryside location where a new development hurt local house prices.

In 2021, construction began on a new development of 254 homes called Bingham Gate in the market town of Bingham, Nottinghamshire. Since the project was completed, homes within half a mile of Bingham Gate have decreased in value by 10.1pc, from £379,058 to £340,787. Meanwhile, properties further away increased in value by 0.7pc to £362,525.

After Yew Tree Park came to Burscough, West Lancs, house prices nearby dropped by 3.2pc, whereas homes more than half a mile away increased in value by 4.5pc.

It is a similar story in Bourne Springs, a development in South Kesteven, Lincs, where local house prices fell by 6.9pc, and Whittlesey Park in Fenland, Cambs, where existing property values dropped by 4.1pc. Prices also fell in the wider area but not by as much.

Labour has pledged to build 1.5 million homes by the end of this parliament yet the Government has so far failed to meet its yearly target of 300,000.

In fact, housebuilding has continued to decline in the worst slowdown since the pandemic. Rating agency S&P Global’s housebuilding subindex fell to 33.5 in December, the lowest reading since May 2020.

Residential applications for planning permission fell 9pc during Labour’s first year in office, while completions dropped almost 13pc to their lowest 12-month tally in nearly a decade.

But the Government is introducing measures designed to accelerate housebuilding. In November the Ministry of Housing announced that housebuilding near well-connected railway stations will be given the green light by default.

Former housing secretary Angela Rayner’s hallmark Planning and Infrastructure Bill finally became law in December. In bad news for rural homeowners and their house prices, her successor Steve Reed has said this means property developers will be “getting spades in the ground faster” from now on.

data.title: Overworked staff to blame for ghost train fiasco, says regulator
data.description:
Overworked staff were to blame for a timetabling blunder that left empty “ghost trains” running from Manchester to London, the head of Britain’s rail regulator said.

John Larkinson, the chief executive of the Office of Rail and Road (ORR), told MPs that the department was too “stretched” by its workload to consider every application to run trains.

This led the ORR to refuse Avanti West Coast permission to operate a 7am passenger service from Manchester Piccadilly to London Euston – even though the train had to leave at that time to be available for a 9.30am departure from the capital to Glasgow.

The decision meant that a fully crewed, 600-seat Pendolino train stood at Piccadilly with its doors locked before heading south in full view of passengers, who were left to wait before cramming on to later services.

Uproar over the “ghost trains” led Peter Hendy, the rail minister, and Heidi Alexander, the Transport Secretary, to intervene to force Mr Larkinson to reinstate the 7am service – with Ms Alexander saying that “passengers were rightly baffled” by the ORR’s original decision.

However, in a letter to the Commons transport select committee, Mr Larkinson pointed to a clash between the ORR and Network Rail as an underlying cause of the fiasco.

Growing tensions

ORR staff had inherited a huge workload of timetable changes dumped on it by the track operator, which refused to carry out its usual role of allocating train routes, he claimed.

Since the ORR lacked the specialist resources of the far larger Network Rail, he wrote, “the whole process was running late,” leaving the regulator – which would normally act only as a final arbiter – “stretched and trying to close out multiple interacting decisions”.

He told MPs: “Network Rail’s approach to recent applications has been to decline to agree any new access contracts, even if it supports them.

“As a consequence we were working through 82 complex and competing track access applications relating to the December 2025 timetable, within constrained timescales.

“Acting as the first-instance decision-maker, rather than as a reviewer, placed greater demands than usual on us.”

The comments hint at growing tensions within the railway industry, as different bodies jostle for position before nationalisation.

Network Rail will be merged with train operators to create the new Great British Railways, while the ORR is expected to see its powers diminished, occupying more of an advisory role.

Mr Larkinson, who is leaving the ORR after 20 years in April, said the ORR had initially chosen to block the 7am train to create a “firebreak” between services that would help cope with any delays to crucial morning services.

He admitted that the decision was a mistake but said staff had understood that the empty train would run to London from its depot without a crew and fitting in between other services.

While Avanti later notified the ORR that the train would in fact run empty from Piccadilly, this was not “escalated appropriately”, he said.

A spokesman for Network Rail said it provided detailed recommendations on all 82 route applications that were before the ORR, including the Manchester departure, which it had proposed should be approved

data.title: Chinese workers rush to buy botched toy that embodies ‘corporate slavery’
data.description:
A sad, frowning horse that represents “the spirit of today’s corporate slaves” has become an unlikely bestselling toy in China.

The forlorn animal, produced to ring in the year of the horse at upcoming Lunar New Year celebrations in February, was supposed to be smiling in the original design.

But a number of them were accidentally produced with sad faces after a worker mistakenly sewed their mouths upside down.

The producer of the toys said she initially offered a refund when she discovered the mistake.

Instead of returning the toys, customers were delighted with them and they quickly sold out.

“A lot of customers like it, and they said it makes sense: that it suits the spirit of today’s corporate slaves,” said Zhang Huoqing, the owner of Happy Sister, a shop in the eastern city of Yiwu that produces the horses.

“This crying horse really fits the reality of modern working people,” she told Reuters.

“People joked that the crying horse is how you look at work, while the smiling one is how you look after work.”

She said the toys went viral after a customer bought one of the horses and a picture of it started circulating online.

Now, her factory has increased its production to meet demands.

In Yiwu International Trade City, China’s largest wholesale market, customers have been witnessed crowding into shops to buy a “crying horse” toy of their own.

‘Emotional value’

The horse is red to symbolise good luck and has the phrase “may you get rich quickly” embroidered in gold on its body. The toy wears a golden collar around its neck and sells for about 25 yuan (£2.62), according to the South China Morning Post.

It follows the trend for “ugly-cute” toys after the widespread success of the Labubu monsters last year, small creatures that wore wicked grins with sharp teeth.

However, for some sellers, the success of the horse has come as a surprise. Lou Zhenxian, a vendor in Yiwu, said she thought the toy was ugly, but provided the “emotional value” that young people were looking for.

“These days, almost everyone who walks through the door asks for the crying horse,” she said.

data.title: Bidders for bust broadband provider put off by rats’ taste for fibre
data.description:
A potential rescue bid for the bust London broadband provider G.Network has been ruled out over fears that rats have chewed through its fibre-optic cables.

Community Fibre, a rival network builder in the capital, has been considering an acquisition of G.Network from administrators, who were called in last month when its £300m of debts and broadband subscriber base of just 25,000 forced it under.

However, Community Fibre chief executive Graeme Oxby told The Telegraph the rescue would not go ahead, partly because of the cost of repairing rodent damage to cables.

“Rodents like ducts and they like fibres which are very tasty,” he said, adding the prospect of costly work to fix the damage had partly deterred a rescue of G.Network by his company.

“It’s not something we’ve been particularly interested in because we think it’s got quite a lot of structural issues and would be quite an expensive fix,” he added.

Rats are known to attack underground fibre-optic cables in search of nesting material and in some cases have been blamed for internet connections being disrupted.

Mr Oxby said his company had not carried out a technical assessment of G.Network and that there were other factors driving the decision not to bid.

Openreach blamed nesting rats for days worth of network issues in Tring two years ago, with engineers discovering rats had chewed through strong ducting, outer casing and multiple cables – which typically require a drill to penetrate.

Residents and businesses in Doncaster were also hit with rat-related broadband issues last month, with local MP and Energy Secretary Ed Miliband saying the issues were caused by “rodent damage”.

He said he had “asked [Openreach] what they’re going to do to prevent widespread outages like this in future”.

Broadband companies such as Openreach typically seal openings to cables to try to prevent rats accessing them. They can also re-route cables to avoid nests.

This is a problem for G.Network because it has laid much of its broadband cables in ducts located in the middle of busy London road, rather than under pavements like its rivals.

Industry sources said this meant the company was required to dig up the road to carry out rodent-linked repair work, leading to costly and disruptive closures.

Residents in areas of the capital including Islington have previously complained of lengthy disruption as a result of G.Network’s installation works.

G.Network was plunged into administration earlier this month just a week after it was acquired by the private equity firm FitzWalter Capital.

FitzWalter, a so-called “vulture fund” that specialises in distressed assets, will now seek to sell off G.Network’s fibre broadband network and its customer base.

The broadband firm is one of dozens of so-called “alt-net” broadband firms that have sprung up to challenge BT but found themselves under strain from surging interest costs and poor take-up.

The company was previously owned by the USS pension fund and Cube Infrastructure Managers before it fell into trouble.

Analysts have predicted a wave of mergers and collapses across the alt-net sector as startup firms struggle under the weight of their debts.

In addition to G.Network, rural broadband firm Gigaclear is poised to be taken over by lenders including Natwest and the taxpayer-backed National Wealth Fund.

But Mr Oxby played down the significance of consolidation, insisting that some alt-nets could survive to challenge BT’s dominance.

He said: “Clearly there are going to be the distressed consolidations, maybe lender-led or specialist-led, but we don’t feel that consolidation is the only answer.

“Recently it’s seemed that alt-nets only exist to consolidate. We set up to be successful competitors to the incumbent and introduce some competition into the market... I think that’s got lost a bit.”

Community Fibre grew its customer base to 429,000 last year, an increase of more than a quarter, and revenue rose by almost 50pc to £112m.

The broadband firm is owned by a consortium of investors include private equity firm Warburg Pincus and the railway pension scheme Railpen.

data.title: Gary Lineker sells stake in podcast empire to Hollywood mogul
data.description:
Gary Lineker has sold a stake in his podcasting empire to a US media group founded by Hollywood mogul Peter Chernin.

Goalhanger, the production company behind The Rest Is ... podcast series, has sold a minority stake to The Chernin Group (TCG) for an undisclosed sum.

Media analysts said Goalhanger could be worth around £100m, based on a three-fold multiple of the company’s £36m turnover last year.

The deal, which is Goalhanger’s first external investment, comes as the company looks to transform its hit podcasts into global franchises across different formats. TCG will also take a seat on Goalhanger’s board.

Goalhanger, which was founded by Mr Lineker alongside former ITV executive Tony Pastor and sports journalist Jack Davenport, last year struck a deal to turn The Rest is Football into a TV show airing on Netflix during this summer’s World Cup.

The company has also enjoyed huge success through shows such as The Rest is History, The Rest is Politics, and The Rest is Entertainment, which have built up large audiences and hosted sell-out shows in venues such as the Royal Albert Hall.

The London-based firm, which employs around 80 people, has now passed more than a quarter of a million paying subscribers, equating to around £15m in annual revenues.

Last year, it generated more than 750 million full-episode views and streams across its network.

Bosses are now targeting the US for further expansion to cash in on the growing demand for its podcasts and live shows across the Atlantic.

The production company has recently struck a number of high-profile editorial partnerships in the US, including a Rest is History series on the history of Disney and a Beatles special with the American talk-show host Conan O’Brien as a special guest.

Goalhanger is also hoping to further cash in on its intellectual property (IP) in areas such as merchandising and publishing.

Los Angeles-based TCG is a media-focused investment firm that has backed start-ups including Barstool Sports, The Athletic and Reese Witherspoon’s production company Hello Sunshine.

It is also a key investor in Substack, the San Francisco-based newsletter publishing platform that has built up 50 million active users.

It was co-founded by Peter Chernin, a former Murdoch executive who runs The North Road, the production company behind Love is Blind and New Girl.

Mr Chernin has previously been linked to potential takeover bids for ITV Studios and All3Media, the British production giant behind The Traitors.

Following TCG’s investment, Goalhanger co-founders Tony Pastor, Jack Davenport and Gary Lineker said: “Since we first met Peter and the TCG team more than a year ago, it has become increasingly clear that they share our vision for what Goalhanger can become, and are the perfect partner to help us realise that ambition.

“This allows us to accelerate our US expansion, develop new IP from our hit shows, and take our ideas into new formats, platforms and markets. With a record number of paying members, we’re building a next-generation media company rooted in trust, creativity and curiosity and this partnership marks an exciting new chapter in that journey.”

Greg Bettinelli, partner at TCG, added: “We are thrilled to be able to work with Tony, Jack and Gary as well as their amazingly talented team. Goalhanger is truly one of the world’s most compelling emerging media businesses and we can’t wait for the rest of their historical journey.”

data.title: Trump sues New York Times over negative opinion poll
data.description:
Donald Trump announced that he will sue The New York Times for publishing a negative opinion poll about his leadership.

“The Times Siena poll... will be added to my lawsuit against the failing New York Times,” Mr Trump said on his Truth Social platform. “They will be held fully responsible for all of their radical Left lies and wrongdoing!”

Mr Trump reacted after the publication of a New York Times/Siena University poll showing his approval rating at just 40 per cent, a figure which is in line with multiple other surveys pointing to declining support a year into his second term.

The US president also suggested that what he called “fake” surveys should be criminalised.

“Fake and fraudulent polling should be, virtually, a criminal offence,” he wrote.

Trump sues multiple media companies

Mr Trump has launched a string of defamation lawsuits against media companies, including the BBC, CNN, Wall Street Journal, CBS and ABC, with some ending in multi-million-dollar settlements.

He first sued The New York Times for $15bn (£11.1bn) in September 2025 for defamation, alleging the paper published false reports to damage his 2024 presidential campaign and reputation.

A federal judge dismissed the case, but it was refiled in amended form a month later.

The Times responded by calling that new lawsuit “an attempt to stifle independent reporting” and “intimidation”.

The Times/Siena poll is widely regarded as one of the most accurate and influential of US political surveys. Its latest findings were notable for what the paper described as the unravelling of Trump’s winning 2024 coalition.

It found that less than a third of voters think the US is better off than it was when Mr Trump returned to the White House a year ago, with a wide majority saying he has focused on the wrong issues.

It also found that a majority of voters disapprove of how Mr Trump has handled top issues including the economy, immigration, the war between Russia and Ukraine and his actions in Venezuela. And significantly, a majority of Americans, 51 per cent, said that Mr Trump’s policies had made life less affordable for them.

Only 42 per cent of voters rated Mr Trump’s first year as a success.

Trump has previously targeted pollsters. In December 2024, he sued Iowa pollster Ann Selzer, accusing her of consumer fraud and “brazen election interference” after she published a poll showing Kamala Harris leading him by three points in Iowa. He dropped the federal lawsuit in June 2025.

data.title: Millions of households to face higher water bills
data.description:
Millions of households are facing higher-than-expected water bills after the competition watchdog allowed five utility companies to increase their charges.

Following a provisional ruling by the Competition and Markets Authority (CMA), Anglian Water, Northumbrian Water, South East Water, Southern Water and Wessex Water will be able to increase bills by a collective £556m over the next five years.

While that is less than the £2.7bn requested by the companies, it will still lead to some households paying annual bills of up to £638 a year.

Emma Hardy, the minister for water and flooding, said she understood the public’s anger over bill rises, but expected companies to offer support to those struggling to pay.

Between them, Anglian and Northumbrian have been allowed to raise bills by a further 1pc, while Southern, South East and Wessex have been handed increases of 3pc, 4pc and 5pc, respectively.

Together, they serve around seven million customers across the UK.

The ruling means that Anglian will charge an average bill of £599, while Northumbrian bill payers will pay £495. South East Water will increase bills to £322, while Southern will charge £638.

Wessex was handed the biggest increase of 5pc, meaning its bills will rise to £622. All had asked for larger increases.

Appeal to the CMA

It comes after the companies appealed to the CMA earlier this year, requesting permission to increase their bills higher than Ofwat, the water regulator, had previously ruled.

In defence of its decision, the CMA said the extra money would help reduce pollution and strengthen supply, although it also said it would help cover the companies’ rising debt costs.

Kirstin Baker, who chaired an independent group of experts appointed by the CMA, said: “We’ve found that water companies’ requests for significant bill increases, on top of those allowed by Ofwat, are largely unjustified.

“We understand the real pressure on household budgets and have worked to keep increases to a minimum, while still ensuring there is funding to deliver essential improvements at a reasonable cost.”

Thames Water, Britain’s biggest water company, had also appealed, but its proceedings have been put on hold while it battles to stave off nationalisation.

Ms Hardy said efforts were being made to restore the public’s trust in water companies.

She said: “We’ve made sure that investment cash goes into infrastructure upgrades, not bonuses, and we’re creating a tough new regulator to clean up our waterways and restore trust in the system.”

It comes amid growing anger over the state of the water sector, which has been responsible for a rise in sewage failures in recent years.

Mike Keil, the chief executive of the Consumer Council for Water (CCW), said: “Many customers are still trying to absorb the impact of April’s unprecedented rise in water bills, so any further increases will be very unwelcome.

“CCW’s own analysis suggests there was an opportunity to reduce these five companies’ financing costs, cutting bills by around £41 a year, but instead the CMA has chosen to increase their rate of return.

“There is a danger the customers of these companies will end up paying more, without seeing any additional improvements in return.”

Joanna Elson, the chief executive of Independent Age, a charity, said higher bills would be “extremely damaging to people on low incomes living in these areas”.

She said: “The people in later life we support tell us the drastic lengths they are taking to reduce their water costs, including only washing a couple of times a week, not flushing the toilet and cutting down on laundry. This shouldn’t be happening in modern Britain.”

Ms Elson added: “We urge the UK Government to protect customers on low incomes from rising water bills by establishing a national water social tariff. This will end the postcode lottery.

 “It’s not right that your address dictates how much financial support you receive.”

data.title: Burnham’s skyscraper tycoon takes £40m dividend after taxpayer loan
data.description:
A skyscraper tycoon backed by Andy Burnham took £40m in dividends from a taxpayer-backed project in Manchester, The Telegraph can reveal.

Daren Whitaker moved the money over a period of three years from WB Developments, a company backed by Greater Manchester Combined Authority (GMCA).

It was received by another company owned by Mr Whitaker.

The disclosures, contained in Companies House filings, will heighten scrutiny of the relationship between the GMCA and Mr Whitaker, who has received more than £700m in taxpayer loans to build luxury skyscrapers in Manchester.

Under a loan agreement seen by The Telegraph, Mr Whitaker’s WB Developments was barred from paying dividends “except with the prior written consent of the lender”.

The dividends were awarded in instalments between 2017 and 2019 from WB Developments, which received £42.5m in cash from the GMCA to help build Wilburn Basin, a 491-bedroom apartment block in Salford.

The first was a £17.7m dividend in 2017, followed by £15.4m and £6.8m in payments in 2018 and 2019.

Mr Whitaker is the ultimate owner of WB Developments, according to filings on Companies House, which falls within his Renaker empire.

The payments were made despite restrictions on dividend payments contained in the initial £42.5m loan agreement between the GMCA, which is chaired by the Mayor of Greater Manchester, and Mr Whitaker for Wilburn Basin. It was struck in 2016 before Mr Burnham was elected Mayor of Greater Manchester.

Asked about the dividends last night, the GMCA refused to say whether officials were aware of the payments or if they granted approval.

The loan was to be repaid in July 2018, two years after construction started on Wilburn Basin.

A spokesman for the GMCA said: “The loan in question was repaid in full, with interest, eight years ago. It was repaid nine months before the deadline, bringing significant benefits to the taxpayer.”

The loans from the GMCA to Mr Whitaker have prompted a High Court claim from rival developer Aubrey Weis. He has alleged Renaker was able to cash in on Manchester’s building boom by unfairly accessing public funds.

Mr Weis has argued that the loans were effectively subsidies for Renaker, which used the cash to build tower blocks that contained no affordable housing. Renaker has not commented and the GMCA has consistently denied the allegations.

A ruling handed down by the Competition Appeal Tribunal last year rejected Mr Weis’s claims.

He is appealing against the decision.

Mr Burnham has sought to defend the loans, telling the BBC last month that the GMCA supported Renaker because “London-based lenders wouldn’t give us the money”.

“They had this downer on Manchester,” he said.

The Telegraph revealed late last year that Mr Whitaker had listed his main residence as Monaco on Companies House. However, this was later changed back to Britain.

When asked about Mr Whitaker’s residency last month, Mr Burnham denied knowledge of his circumstances but said: “I can’t comment on people’s decisions about their lives. People have got to be free to where they live.

“It’s not the case that someone has walked away with anything other than having invested in our city and built it out. The towers are helping us to bring investors to the city.”

After building a string of different skyscrapers, Renaker is now pursuing its most ambitious project to date.

This is called the Lighthouse, a skyscraper consisting of 642 high-end flats with a restaurant on its top floor.

Renaker was contacted for comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Turbines waste enough wind power to supply all of London’s homes
data.description:
Britain’s wind farm turbines wasted enough energy to power all of London’s homes last year, new figures show.

A record 10 terawatt hours (TWh) of wind power went to waste in 2025, according to a report from energy analyst Montel – costing billpayers a total of £1.4bn in “curtailment costs”.

This was up 22pc on the year before, as growing strain on the grid prevented wind power from being transported to the cities and towns that need it most.

This results in so-called curtailment costs, which are paid to wind farms when they are asked to switch off.

At the same time, grid operators must call on gas plants to step in and keep the lights on with replacement power, often at great expense.

Analysts said that wind farms in Scotland were largely to blame.

Montel’s report said: “The amount of renewable electricity curtailed in Great Britain in 2025 (10TWh) could have met the combined electricity demand of every domestic household in London for the entire year.

“Northern Scotland has seen the most curtailment by far. Over 8.8TWh of wind power in northern Scotland was switched off, enough to also power all Scottish domestic electricity demand for the year.”

The cost of curtailment has been growing as Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary, rolls out more renewable energy sources – such as wind and solar.

As well as paying wind developers, Montel said solar farms are also being asked to switch off because of bottlenecks.

Montel said: “Solar curtailment costs rose over the year to total over £252,000. While this is substantially lower than the corresponding figures for wind, it represents a rise from the negligible costs associated with solar curtailment in 2024.”

To combat curtailment, regulator Ofgem has approved plans for the UK’s three transmission operators – National Grid, Scottish Power and SSE – to spend up to £90bn on new lines and substations.

However, these will take at least five years to build, with constraint payments set to continue rising.

Octopus Energy has warned that such payments could see £8bn added to bills by 2030.

Claire Coutinho, the shadow energy secretary, has blamed Mr Miliband’s planned expansion of renewables for the recent uptick in costs.

The Energy Secretary has approved large numbers of new wind and solar projects as part of his plan to decarbonise the grid by 2030.

She said: “We are paying more than ever before to pay wind farms to switch off when the wind blows.

“Costs are set to triple by 2030 as he approves more wind farms than ever before. He cares far more about his own Clean Power 2030 target than looking after consumers.”

A spokesman for SSE said: “ The UK has built renewables where the resources are strongest, and now we need to upgrade the grid to eliminate bottlenecks so we can harness more of that energy, more of the time.”

The National Energy System Operator said it was working to keep energy costs down but stressed that grid expansion was crucial.

A spokesman said: “Without the crucial expansion of the electricity network by those who own it, and industry working together to deliver the changes needed, there is a risk that curtailment costs will continue to rise.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: ‘I was quoted £4k a year home insurance for my chocolate box thatch cottage’
data.description:
When public sector sales director Russell Kirby, 63, and his wife, Sarah, first saw their thatched cottage in the village of Dunkeswell in East Devon, they couldn’t contain their excitement.

They had been living in a detached 1950s house in Lindford, Hampshire, where Russell hadn’t really felt at home.

All that changed when they saw the four-bed chocolate box cottage with roses around the door and a large garden, perfect for their four dogs.

After putting in their best offer of £530,000, the pair were overjoyed when it was accepted in summer 2023.

However, one thing they hadn’t factored in was the enormous cost of building insurance, which for their new Grade II-listed home was quoted – at one point – at just shy of £4,000 a year.

A survey completed in their second year of ownership in 2025 put the home’s rebuild cost (how much it would cost to reinstate if destroyed) at just over £1m, which sent their building insurance spiralling.

The same thing is happening to other thatched property owners, with some saying they feel they are being held to ransom by insurers. One owner in Devon said their premium for a three-bed semi-detached Grade II-listed home had almost tripled, from £470 in 2018 to £1,300 this year.

Thatched homes tend to have higher insurance premiums, as fire damage is often severe and rebuilding is a long, specialised process.

But homeowners have told The Telegraph that they are now concerned that traditional thatched cottages will disappear altogether as people choose to tile non-listed properties, rather than re-thatch them, to make them easier to sell.

“My wife really wanted the house; both of us fell in love with it,” says. “I’d holidayed nearby as a child, so there were a lot of happy memories linked to the area.

“This home is my children’s inheritance, but because of these sky-high insurance costs, I do worry that some of these houses could become impossible to sell.”

The Kirbys had to get building insurance to exchange contracts on the house.

It was a probate sale, and the previous owner had insured through the National Farmers Union (NFU) Mutual, which was also the couple’s insurer at the time.

Their old home had cost them about £600 a year for both buildings and contents insurance.

Russell assumed that switching to his new home would incur an increase; it was a Grade II-listed building, after all. But he was not prepared for what that increase would be.

As the exchange loomed, Russell approached the NFU Mutual for a quote and was asked about the rebuild value. He did not know, so he just told them the home’s value of £530,000.

His quote came out at £2,808 a year, and the couple was so close to completion that they had no choice but to take the NFU Mutual up on it.

There were also strict conditions: no open flames within 100m of the house, so no barbecues, garden fires or patio heaters.

Their boiler and Aga cooker had to be serviced annually, and both chimneys in use had to be swept every 12 months, adding a further cost of around £500 a year on top of insurance.

Then, two years into living in their dream home, an NFU surveyor visited the house to audit the fire extinguishers, another condition of the policy.

“They asked again about the rebuild cost, and we had to have a survey,” Russell says. “It turned out we had been massively underinsured, and the cost of rebuilding the house was actually £1,000,224.”

New calculations then saw his premiums leap to £3,998 a year.

Having the wrong rebuild estimate could put millions of other homeowners in peril.

According to Rebuild Cost Assessment, as of September 2025, only 7pc of UK properties are correctly insured, with the average underinsured home covered for just 67pc of its actual rebuild cost.

That means that if your home is completely destroyed, you may not receive enough to rebuild it, or you may receive nothing at all.

A landscape of rising costs

According to the Association of British Insurers (ABI), the annual average price of combined building and contents insurance in the second quarter of 2025 was £391.

This rises to an average of £648 for a Grade I property and £555 for a Grade II building, according to the latest available Confused.com data from the last quarter of 2024.

Those figures may already be out of date – experts say rebuild costs have shot up massively since Brexit, Covid and the war in Ukraine.

An ABI spokesperson said: “Listed buildings or properties with unique constructions often carry additional and specific risks that require tailored insurance. For example, thatched cottages can present a higher fire risk.”

According to broker Howden, post-pandemic material cost rises have now stabilised, but new environmental legislation and climbing labour costs are pushing rebuild prices up.

The construction workforce is down by over 365,000 workers compared to 2008, and a third of skilled tradespeople – particularly relevant to listed buildings – are set to retire within the next decade.

Russell says the monthly payments were not crippling to him and his wife, Sarah, 56, a personal assistant, while they were working, but the cost would have been unaffordable after retirement.

Russell eventually shopped around and secured a much more affordable quote of £1,494 a year for both buildings and contents.

Tom Chapman, of The Home Insurer, a non-standard broker that now insures the Kirbys via underwriter Aviva, says owners should consider increasing their excess, removing wood-burning stoves, increasing their chimney height and ensuring their contents are not over-insured to bring down premiums.

An NFU Mutual spokesman said: “Thatched homes present unique risks and are often made of non-standard materials, which, if needed, will require specialist repairs, which can be significantly more expensive than the standard.”

They said his insurance increased by less than 7pc between 2023 and 2025, and the latest renewal saw a larger rise, since his sums insured increased by over 80pc.

data.title: Higher water bills are a shameless reward for failure
data.description:
On the face of it, the news that water suppliers won’t be allowed to ramp up bills by as much as they were hoping is cause for celebration.

At first glance, it looks like an all-too-rare rebuff for an industry that has been thumbing its nose at customers for far too long – which is, of course, precisely how this latest sorry charade is meant to be interpreted.

Ministers are desperate for us all to think that after decades of wrongdoing, it has taken a Labour government to finally get tough with the cowboys that pollute our rivers and charge us a fortune for the privilege, all while absentee shareholders and negligent executives line their own pockets with money that could’ve been spent on investment.

Yet, that isn’t what is happening here. Not even close.

Yes, bills won’t be increasing to the extent that suppliers were asking for – but the point is that an independent panel of “experts” has still sanctioned further rises beyond what Ofwat had approved and most objective observers thought those rises were already pretty steep.

So the idea that this is somehow a big victory for households and a defeat for water companies is profound nonsense. The opposite is true.

In fact, it is impossible to see further bill hikes as anything other than a reward for failure for an industry that has long treated customers and the environment with complete contempt.

Not that Emma Hardy, the minister for water and flooding, seems even remotely prepared to acknowledge what is really happening here.

“We are laser-focused on helping ease the cost-of-living pressure on households. We’ve frozen fuel duty, raised the minimum wage and pensions and brought down mortgage rates, putting more money in people’s pockets,” she said – though hopefully not with a straight face.

This is gaslighting territory. It takes a special kind of shamelessness to boast about making people better off at the precise moment that millions of families are having a fresh round of bill rises imposed on them.

The Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) said on Thursday it is allowing five water companies – Anglian Water, Northumbrian Water, Southern Water, South East Water and Wessex Water – to lift bills by up to 5pc over the next five years.

That might seem fairly tame if it wasn’t for the fact that it comes on top of an average increase of 24pc that industry regulator Ofwat had already approved in April.

As Mike Keil, chief executive of the Consumer Council for Water (CCW), pointed out: “Many customers are still trying to absorb the impact of April’s unprecedented rise in water bills, so any further increases will be very unwelcome.”

In fact, two in five of the households these companies serve say they will struggle to afford the increases coming down their pipe, according to the CCW, which represents water customers across England and Wales.

What’s more, the CCW has conducted its own analysis showing there was an opportunity to reduce these five companies’ financing costs to cut bills by around £41 a year – “but instead the CMA has chosen to increase their rate of return,” Keil said.

However, you would’ve thought otherwise when reading Hardy’s hollow words on Thursday.

“I understand the public anger over bill rises – that’s why I expect every water company to offer proper support to anyone struggling to pay,” she said.

But as Keil said: “Three of these five companies also have no plans to end water poverty by 2030.”

The CMA doesn’t come out of this much better. It makes a big song and dance about how the additional funding will ensure “essential improvements”, such as more resilient supply and a reduction in pollution, are delivered “at reasonable cost”.

Most people will accept some level of inflation in their household expenses but I’m willing to bet that few regard a 27pc surge as reasonable – even if it is over five years.

Some customers are already grappling with rises close to, or more than, 50pc once inflation is added because suppliers have been outrageously front-loading increases to help pay for long-overdue infrastructure improvements.

The reality is that bill-payers are being asked to subsidise an era of playing catch-up because water suppliers failed in the past to keep pace with investment, maintenance and environmental duties.

The money was there but instead of being spent modernising the system, repairing antiquated pipes and ensuring sewage wasn’t pouring into our waterways, it was being lavished on greedy investors and weak-kneed bosses or vanishing straight out the door in the form of heavy debt repayments.

Research from the University of Greenwich has found that 35p in every pound spent on customer bills is swallowed up in debt and dividend costs.

With the system buckling, it is surely no coincidence that these are the highest bill increases since privatisation.

It is also no surprise that the suppliers that went crying to the CMA are also among the most debt-laden in a sector that has long been leveraged up to the gills.

Meanwhile, the competition policeman quietly concedes that alongside a much-trumpeted round of additional investment, bill increases will also help pay down debt and boost returns for investors – many of whom are the same faceless funds that presided over the current wretched state of affairs in the first place.

For all the talk of long overdue reform and a new era of genuine accountability from Labour, it’s hard to avoid the conclusion that our broken water industry has effectively been handed a huge taxpayer-funded bailout.

data.title: Give under-22s free bus travel, says Green Party
data.description:
The Green Party is calling for free bus travel in England for those under the age of 22.

Zack Polanski, the party’s leader, said introducing the universal bus pass for the group could help young people shut out of employment and training by the cost of living.

The party said the scheme would be self-financing, arguing that increased bus usage would boost the economy.

Mr Polanski said: “Young people are increasingly shut out of employment and training by the cost of living crisis, which often hits the youngest the hardest.

“This Labour Government has made a dire situation for young people even worse with the poorest getting even poorer and rich getting even richer.

“Free bus travel for the young is one part of improving the lives of younger people and showing what a richer and greener country looks like if we invest in people and not hand over our assets to the super-rich and corporations.”

The scheme would bring England into line with Scotland, where those aged five to 22 have been entitled to free bus travel since 2022.

Under-18s in London already travel for free on buses with other discounts available for some up to the age of 25, while some bus companies across England also offer discounts for young people.

Mr Polanski said Green Party councillors, if elected in May’s local polls, will seek to bring in the universal bus scheme in their areas.

He said: “Green councillors elected in May will be using their influence to implement this scheme in their areas and we will be applying political pressure at a national level for a scheme recommended by the transport select committee last year to be made real.

“Free bus travel for the young is part of our mission to make hope normal again.”

The party pointed to research by KPMG estimating that 2.2 million commuters rely on buses to get to work and generate £72bn in collective income each year.

Anyone at state pension age in Britain can currently claim a national older person’s bus pass.

In London those over 60 can get an Oyster card with free travel around the capital.

data.title: Billionaire former Tory donor pays himself £477m
data.description:
A billionaire former Tory donor paid himself £477m last year as revenues soared at his City hedge fund.

Chris Rokos cemented his position as one of Britain’s richest men, and is believed to be the highest-paid partner at his eponymous fund, Rokos Capital Management, which more than doubled its turnover from £445m to £1.2bn.

The fund’s latest accounts revealed that the total pool of profits for its 23 partners rose to a record £940m in the 12 months to March, up from £315m the year prior.

While the results do not disclose the identity of the highest-paid partner, Mr Rokos is widely considered the fund’s top earner, who founded the firm in 2015 and is its majority owner.

Mr Rokos, who is one of Britain’s leading hedge fund managers, was also previously a donor to the Conservatives, giving the party around £2m between 2009 and 2015. He made his last donation in 2018.

He also owns the centuries-old, Grade I listed Tottenham House, one of England’s grandest stately homes. Earlier this year, Mr Rokos made headlines by applying for planning permission to install around 1,100 solar panels to provide power to the 100-bed mansion.

Under the plans, two acres of the manor estate – currently used for silage and grazing dairy cows – would be developed to house solar panels. These would provide nearly one third of the power required to run the estate.

Tottenham House was part of the Savernake Estate, once a royal hunting forest with significant links to Henry VIII. The family of Jane Seymour, the King’s third wife, took ownership of the home before passing it down through several generations.

Mr Rokos has been working to restore the house into a family home.

He has launched a series of ambitious construction projects, such as underground tunnels connecting the main house to the stables. He has also installed a tennis pavilion and a cinema.

Before launching Rokos Capital, he also co-founded Brevan Howard, another City hedge fund.

Unlike most large hedge funds, which have multiple portfolio managers making bets on the market, Mr Rokos is the main trader at Rokos Capital.

He is supported by a team of analysts and senior investment professionals, which the firm calls investment officers.

Rokos Capital Management was contacted for comment.

data.title: Google forced to end AI ‘exploitation’ of websites
data.description:
Google will be forced to change its search engine after its artificial intelligence (AI) technology was accused of “exploiting” websites against their will.

In a crackdown announced on Wednesday, the Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) said Google would have to let companies choose whether to appear in the AI summaries at the top of search results.

The US tech giant will also be barred from “manipulating” its search results by retaliating against companies that criticise it or favouring businesses that spend heavily on Google advertising. Google denies engaging in such practices.

The restrictions, which could come into force in the next year, would signal the first action under new competition laws designed to address big tech’s dominance.

The News Media Association (NMA) – which represents titles including The Times, The Guardian and The Telegraph and has been pushing for a crackdown – said Google “exploits a dominant position in search”.

Google, traditionally known for providing links to other websites, has increasingly placed AI overviews at the top of search results.

The overviews, based on information scraped from websites, are designed to answer search queries without users visiting websites and have been blamed for significant drops in people visiting the websites themselves.

The CMA said website owners were unable to opt out of their content being used for AI overviews without blocking Google – a key source of internet traffic – entirely.

Under the watchdog’s proposals, Google will have to let publishers choose whether their sites can power AI overviews.

It will also be obliged to attribute clearer information to the original source, while also requiring Google’s search rankings to be “fair and transparent”.

Exploitation and discrimination claims

Publishers have claimed that the company prioritises search results from sites with which it has commercial relationships and demotes those that criticise it in public.

“Google should adopt non-discriminatory and objective criteria to mitigate the risk that Google manipulates the ranking and presentation of search results based on irrelevant and unfair considerations,” the CMA said in a consultation document.

Google has denied the claims and the CMA said it was hard to verify the complaints.

Owen Meredith, the NMA’s chief executive, said: “Google’s current approach exploits their dominant position in search by forcing publishers to opt out of appearing in search results entirely – reducing public’s access to trusted sources of news – unless they are willing to give their content away for free to AI chatbots that are then ranked above their own journalism.”

However, he said the CMA should do more to block Google’s web crawlers from being used to train AI models.

Ron Eden, of Google, said: “We look forward to engaging in the CMA’s process and will continue discussions with website owners and other stakeholders on this topic.”

However, he warned that “any new controls need to avoid breaking search in a way that leads to a fragmented or confusing experience for people”.

Sarah Cardell, the CMA’s chief executive, said: “These targeted and proportionate actions would give UK businesses and consumers more choice and control over how they interact with Google’s search services – as well as unlocking greater opportunities for innovation across the UK tech sector and broader economy.

“They would also provide a fairer deal for content publishers, particularly news organisations, over how their content is used in Google’s AI overviews.”

The CMA is consulting on the measures over the next four weeks before making a final decision. It is then likely to take six months for the changes to come into effect.

data.title: Trump warned credit card crackdown will trigger financial crisis for millions
data.description:
Donald Trump’s crackdown on credit cards risks pushing millions of poor Americans into financial crisis, some of Wall Street’s most powerful groups have warned.

Mr Trump is pressing ahead with plans to temporarily cap credit card interest rates for 12 months at 10pc from January 20.

However, the proposal is facing a backlash over fears it will force banks to cancel cards and pull back lending to consumers with the lowest credit scores.

On Monday, the American Bankers Association (ABA), which represents major lenders like JP Morgan, hedge fund billionaire Bill Ackman and the Bank Policy Institute (BPI) all rounded on Mr Trump over the plan.

Mr Ackman, who has publicly supported Mr Trump, added: “My concern about capping rates at 10pc is that doing so will inevitably cause millions of Americans to have their cards cancelled as credit card companies lose the ability to adequately price subprime credit risk.

“Consumers denied credit cards will be forced to turn to loan sharks whose rates and terms will be vastly worse for borrowers.”

The BPI said the cap would “be devastating for millions of American families and small business owners … the very consumers this proposal intends to help”. The ABA said the move would push consumers towards “less regulated, more costly alternatives”.

Mr Trump’s announcement sent shares in US credit card providers sliding on Monday, with Capital One leading the fallers plunging by as much as 6.24pc.

In the UK, as much as £8bn was wiped off the market value of British banks. Shares in Barclays, which has a US credit card business, fell by 3.3pc in London. Shares in NatWest were also down by 1.2pc, even though the bank does not operate a US credit card business.

The plan has stoked widespread fears because credit cards are so vital for the US economy, with the number of accounts hitting a record 642 million in the third quarter of 2025.

This means the US has nearly twice as many credit card accounts as it does people. Total credit card debt is at a record $1.23tn (£891bn) – up by 39pc since the pandemic began.

In theory, capping at 10pc should dramatically reduce borrowers’ costs because it would more than halve the average rate paid, which was 22.5pc in November.

At no point since at least 1994, when Federal Reserve data began, has the rate been as low as 10pc. According to Vanderbilt University, a 10pc cap would save US consumers $73bn net a year.

However, analysts warn it is precisely the people who Trump is trying to help that will be hit the hardest by his rate cap because it will force banks to do less lending.

Justin Begley, economist at Moody’s Analytics said: “If the government does indeed enforce this policy on the banks, then it’s just going to become completely unprofitable to lend to people that are of higher credit risk.

“I would disagree with people who are saying that the banks can absorb this. The banks have to be able to properly price credit risk.”

Banks may simply cancel subprime borrowers’ credit cards outright, Mr Begley added. “That’s something I have heard banks talking about doing. They’re going to want to protect themselves.”

Jefferies economist Mohit Kumar said: “It is likely that lower rates will push credit providers to reassess their lending criteria and the policy ends up creating more harm than good.”

Subprime borrowers have a credit card default rate of 1.2pc, double the overall default rate, according to Moody’s. Currently, subprime borrowers make up 21pc of accounts in default. During the financial crisis, the rate was closer to half.

The measure will hit at a time when low and middle income US households are becoming increasingly overwhelmed with their debts.

The share of Americans who are falling seriously behind on their credit card payments has already soared to a near 15-year high.

Debt advice firms have been warning that America’s cost of living crisis is increasingly hitting middle class households, as families turn to credit just to cover the costs of basic necessities.

data.title: One in four electric cars sold in Britain are Chinese
data.description:
More than one in four electric vehicles (EVs) sold in the UK are now made in China, as Beijing helps to power Britain’s shift away from petrol and diesel.

Chinese-made vehicles accounted for 27.9pc of the more than 470,000 electric cars sold in the UK last year, according to the Society of Motor Manufacturers and Traders (SMMT).

Across all fuel types, Chinese-made vehicles accounted for a record 13.5pc of the total market last year, or one in eight. Sales rose by more than 50pc amid an aggressive push by brands including BYD, Jaecoo and Omoda to break into the UK.

Sales of BYD cars grew more than fivefold in Britain last year. The Chinese giant overtook Tesla to become the world’s biggest seller of electric cars in 2025.

Even celebrated British brands such as MG now count as made in China after falling under foreign ownership. Sweden-headquartered EV brand Polestar also manufacture in the country, while some Tesla models are made at the company’s Shanghai plant.

The surge in Chinese EV sales helped electric vehicles to account for 23.4pc of new cars registered in Britain in 2025, rising to 32.3pc in December, the SMMT said.

The figure will be welcomed by Labour ministers, who have vowed to ban the sale of new petrol and diesel cars by 2030 and hybrids by 2035.

However, the growing reliance on China for EVs is likely to prove controversial. Both the European Union and US have cracked down on imports from China, amid concerns about unfair subsidies and possible national security threats.

The US-based Centre for Strategic and International Studies has estimated that the Chinese government spent at least $230bn (£170bn) between 2009 to 2023 supporting its EV industry.

A 100pc tariff on Chinese electric vehicles in the US has effectively closed the market, while the EU has imposed heavy tariffs on imports.

The UK Government has said it has no plans for tariffs on Chinese car imports.

When combined with hybrid vehicles, battery-powered cars now account for just under half of all new car sales, the SMMT said. Plug-in hybrids, which combine a smaller battery with a petrol engine, are the fastest-growing part of the market, rising by 35pc last year.

Sales of fully electric cars were up 24pc, while petrol and diesel cars fell 8pc and 15pc respectively.

However, sales of EVs remained behind the zero emission vehicle mandate, which required 28pc of cars sold in 2025 to be electric.

This means sales were even further behind target than in 2024, when EVs made up 19.6pc of sales compared to a 22pc target. The mandate will rise again this year to a third of total car sales.

Manufacturers who miss the quota can cover the difference by buying credits from those who surpass it, making up sales in later years, or by cutting the CO2 emissions of other cars on the road.

However, manufacturers that are significantly below-target face the prospect of paying a fee of £12,000 per non-compliant car sold.

The SMMT said that manufacturers spent £5.5bn last year on subsidising EV sales in order to come closer to the target, or £11,000 per vehicle. The trade body said this was “unsustainable” and urged ministers to ease the requirements.

“The mandate still challenges the industry to hit levels that are apart from the natural demand that’s there,” said Mike Hawes, the SMMT’s chief executive.

He said a review of the mandate scheduled for 2027 should be brought forward to this year. “The assumptions behind the mandate when they were made, we need to review them.”

The EU has delayed plans to ban combustion engine cars, moving the date from 2035 to 2040. Labour has resisted calls to follow suit.

Overall, sales of new cars rose 3.5pc to 2.02m last year, the highest level since 2019, although they remain some way below pre-Covid levels.

data.title: The battle over ‘mad’ plans to redevelop Liverpool Street station
data.description:
On a cold night this week as the rain lashed down, campaigners gathered in an unassuming east London office block to thwart a £1.5bn revamp of Britain’s busiest station.

The Liverpool Street Station Campaign (Lissca) – a “supergroup” of heritage enthusiasts spearheaded by TV presenter Griff Rhys Jones – is fighting plans to demolish and rebuild key parts of the railway terminus and linked Underground station.

The group this week unveiled its alternative vision, one they claim will help to modernise Liverpool Street station without destroying its history.

The rival plan drawn up by John McAslan + Partners, the architects behind the transformation of King’s Cross station in 2012, will be presented to local officials in the coming weeks.

“If you’re coming from Italy on a train to Liverpool Street station, would you rather see a brand new gateway ... or something which says Victorian, Britain and great railway heritage?” Rhys Jones says.

“If you break it, it’s gone, you can’t get it back. It could be a much richer experience, without doing an awful lot.”

Network Rail say expansion is needed to cope with the expected increase in passenger numbers at a station already at capacity. They argue only a wholesale overhaul will do.

Campaigners, meanwhile, claim that plans to modernise the station would ruin a Victorian landmark and create years of needless disruption.

The fight over Liverpool Street station, which opened fully in 1875, is a microcosm of the tug of war between heritage and modernisation that threatens to hamper Labour’s ambitions for a construction boom.

Since taking power, Sir Keir Starmer has vowed to make it more difficult for people to oppose major building projects.

He has pledged to override the “whims of Nimbys” and back the builders rather than the “blockers”. But in cases like Liverpool Street, these statements have only galvanised the opposition.

Al Watson, head of planning at Taylor Wessing, says: “The situation is more tense now than it was four years ago because the previous government leaned more towards heritage.

“This Government, by its very nature, pre- and post-election, is pro-development. That’s the vision, that’s the instruction, and that’s why it is more tense now than it has been in a long time.”

Until now, Lissca has faced down each proposal to redevelop Liverpool Street station. The group in fact dates back to the 1970s, when British Rail’s initial bid to demolish the terminus was thwarted by Lissca, then fronted by the poet Sir John Betjeman.

More recently, plans drawn up in 2023 by architects Herzog and de Meuron were shot down after the group helped mobilise 2,200 objections and 26,000 petition signatures.

“We’ve won battle after battle,” says Sir Simon Jenkins, founder of the Railway Heritage Trust and a supporter of the group.

The new plans, ordered by Network Rail and designed by architects Acme, will involve partially demolishing the station to expand its concourse.

A 19-storey, 97m glass office tower would be built above a neighbouring listed former hotel, while gothic yellow brick vaults would arch over the station’s entrances.

Over 150 million passengers by 2041

Network Rail argues that expansion is essential.

The opening of the Elizabeth Line, which passes through Liverpool Street, has helped the station become the UK’s busiest in each of the past three years.

There were 98 million rail entries and exits last year, and Network Rail predicts this will grow to 158 million by 2041.

Planning officials for the City of London Corporation are currently reviewing the revamp proposals, with councillors expected to vote on them on Feb 10.

The scheme has so far attracted more than 2,275 letters of objection and 1,135 in favour.

The February deadline has galvanised Lissca into action, with the group hoping their alternative plans will convince the City of London to reject Network Rail’s plans.

“What we have to do over the next 20 days … is do what we can to make sure that [the committee is] aware that the City of London deserves better than this current scheme that is before them, and they have the right to ask for it to be better,” Jones says.

‘Illogical’ plans

While Network Rail’s designs will evoke the station’s Victorian heritage, Lissca believe they can retain far more of the original building.

They estimates its plans will take half the time and cost half as much as Network Rail’s proposals, with almost no demolition required.

“The reason it’s so high is because of the void costs, created by the demolition of so much fabric,” says McAslan. “It’s so illogical, it’s mad.”

Lissca’s vision includes a green walkway inspired by the Eden Project, with Sir Tim Smit, founder of the Eden Project, advising McAslan on landscape strategy.

A vaulted office building would sit at the northern end of the station’s platforms, with nine floors of timber hanging from a lightweight steel frame to provide around 700,000 sq ft of workspace.

McAslan believes Network Rail’s plans will cause 12 years of disruption for travellers – a timeframe he calls “completely unacceptable”.

But Network Rail says this timeframe is a gross exaggeration.

Work on modernising the station itself will begin as early as 2028 and be completed in about five years, with development of offices above the passenger concourse taking three more.

While ticket barriers will be moved and shops demolished to enlarge the concourse, the impact on people using the station will be limited to the “mild inconvenience” of having to use different entrances at certain times, Network Rail says.

Officials argue the project should be no more disruptive than the modernisation of Paddington at the turn of the century and far removed from the upheaval that accompanied the upgrade of London Bridge station.

The five-year, £1bn project to transform London Bridge, the capital’s oldest rail terminus, resulted in delays and dangerous levels of overcrowding.

A Network Rail spokesman said: “It’s very much not going to take 12 years and it won’t be on the scale of London Bridge because none of the work being done is to create new tracks and platforms.”

Network Rail commissioned an independent team to review McAslan’s preliminary scheme but concluded it was undeliverable owing to complexity, funding challenges and the potential for prolonged station closures.

Its review concluded that building offices over the train shed – as proposed in the McAslan plan – would be too costly and structurally challenging because Network Rail does not own land to either side.

It would also require “significant closure” of the station to build a deck over live lines.

Back in the war room at McAslan’s Farringdon offices, campaigners are undeterred.

“There’s a critical mass of buildings which create a sense of the Victorian past in that part of the city, and it must be preserved,” says Sir Simon.

“Just remember, there’s always a choice. Nothing is inevitable. Fight on.”
